commands.review_summary:
The PR adds manual-run isolation to cron result application, preserves scheduled cron counters/state for manual runs, exposes `lastRunWasManual` in cron state schemas, and expands cron regression tests.

PR surface: Source +65, Tests +852. Total +917 across 10 files.

Reproducibility: yes. at source level: current main routes manual completions through `applyJobResult` without a manual discriminator, so `deleteAfterRun` and scheduled-state mutations are reachable from manual runs. I did not run a fresh live Gateway repro in this read-only review.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] Human review is needed for current-head proof and compatibility/API semantics; I did not find a narrow automated code repair to queue.

target.title: fix(cron): skip deleteAfterRun and preserve counters for manual runs (#83538)
target.body:
## Summary

`openclaw cron run` (manual/debug runs) on a `deleteAfterRun` at-job deletes the job on success — consuming the one-shot and preventing its scheduled execution. Manual runs also mutate `consecutiveErrors`/`consecutiveSkipped` counters, interfering with scheduled retry/backoff state.

This PR adds an `isManual` flag to `applyJobResult` so manual runs preserve the job and leave scheduled execution state untouched.

## Root cause

`applyJobResult()` has no concept of manual vs scheduled runs. When a manual run succeeds on a `deleteAfterRun: true` at-job, `shouldDelete` evaluates to `true` and the job is removed from the store — the scheduled fire never happens.

Additionally, manual error/skipped/success outcomes unconditionally mutate `consecutiveErrors` and `consecutiveSkipped` counters, which control retry backoff and auto-disable behavior for scheduled runs.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `timer.ts` | Add `isManual` option to `applyJobResult`; skip `deleteAfterRun` for manual runs; guard all counter mutations and alert emissions with `!isManual` |
| `ops.ts` | Pass `isManual: true` from manual run completion and invalid-persisted-run handling |
| `timer.regression.test.ts` | 5 new tests: manual ok/error preserve job, manual doesn't increment/reset counters |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts
 ✓ cron (45 tests) 144ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  45 passed (45)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` on a `deleteAfterRun` at-job deletes it, preventing scheduled execution. Manual runs also mutate scheduled retry counters.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at `aa92a4a5b0`, macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0.

### Source-level proof

```
Main branch applyJobResult():
  - shouldDelete = kind === "at" && deleteAfterRun && status === "ok"
    → no manual discriminator, always deletes on success
  - consecutiveErrors incremented unconditionally on error
  - consecutiveSkipped incremented unconditionally on skip

Fix branch:
  - shouldDelete = wouldDelete && !opts?.isManual
    → manual runs never trigger deletion
  - Counter mutations guarded: if (!opts?.isManual) { ... }
    → manual runs don't affect retry/backoff state
```

### Regression test coverage

```
✅ manual ok on deleteAfterRun at-job → job preserved, not deleted
✅ manual error on deleteAfterRun at-job → job preserved, not deleted  
✅ scheduled ok on deleteAfterRun at-job → job deleted (unchanged behavior)
✅ manual error does not increment consecutiveErrors (pre-existing=3 → still 3)
✅ manual success does not reset consecutiveErrors (pre-existing=3 → still 3)
```

- **What was not tested**: Live `openclaw cron run` CLI flow (requires a running gateway with a configured `deleteAfterRun` at-job). The `isManual` flag propagation from `ops.ts` to `applyJobResult` is validated at the unit level.

Closes #83538


### Re-verification (2026-05-20)

Local checkout at `d89f14de26`, macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`.

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts

 ✓ cron  ../../src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts (39 tests) 116ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  39 passed (39)
   Duration  152ms
```

Full cron suite (`src/cron/`) also run: 94 test files, 901 passed. The 7 failures are in unrelated files (`run-log.test.ts`, `service.failure-alert.test.ts`) pre-existing on upstream/main.


## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: `openclaw cron run` (manual) of a `deleteAfterRun: true` at-job incorrectly deletes the job. Manual runs also pollute `consecutiveErrors`/`consecutiveSkipped` counters.
- **Real environment tested**: Local patched checkout at `0cec2acc7a`, macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0.
- **Failing proof before fix**: On `upstream/main`, counter mutations and `shouldDelete` run unconditionally — no `isManual` discriminator exists.
- **Exact steps after patch**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts` + `src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts`
- **Evidence after fix**: 46/46 timer regression tests pass (including 7 new #83538/#83933-specific tests). 7/7 failure-alert tests pass.
- **What was not tested**: Live Gateway CLI flow; the tests exercise the same `applyJobResult` code path the Gateway calls internally.

### Key test results

| Scenario | Result |
|----------|--------|
| Manual run + deleteAfterRun + success | NOT deleted ✅ |
| Scheduled run + deleteAfterRun + success | Deleted ✅ |
| Manual error + consecutiveErrors=3 | Stays 3 ✅ |
| Manual success + consecutiveErrors=5 | Stays 5 ✅ |
| Manual error → failure alert | NOT triggered ✅ |
| Scheduled error → failure alert | Triggered ✅ |


## Real behavior proof (Docker, 2026-05-21)

**Environment**: Docker container, `node:24-bookworm-slim` (aarch64), patched source at `885d605e56`.

### Latest fixes

| Issue | Problem | Fix |
|-------|---------|-----|
| P1 | Manual at-job error was disabling job (`enabled=false`, `nextRunAtMs=undefined`) | At-job error retry/disable block now guarded with `if (opts?.isManual)` |
| P2 | Manual success was clearing `lastFailureAlertAtMs` cooldown | Cooldown clearing now inside `if (!opts?.isManual)` guard |

### Test results

| Test Suite | Result |
|------------|--------|
| Timer regression (49 tests) | ✅ All passed |
| Failure-alert (7 tests) | ✅ All passed |

### Key verified behaviors

| Scenario | Result |
|----------|--------|
| Manual at-job error | `enabled` stays true, `nextRunAtMs` preserved ✅ |
| Manual success | `lastFailureAlertAtMs` cooldown preserved ✅ |
| Manual run + deleteAfterRun | Job NOT deleted ✅ |
| Scheduled run + deleteAfterRun | Job deleted ✅ |
| Manual error + consecutiveErrors=3 | Stays 3 ✅ |
| Manual success + consecutiveErrors=5 | Stays 5 ✅ |


## Real CLI proof (Docker Gateway, 2026-05-21)

**Environment**: Docker `node:24-bookworm-slim` (aarch64), patched Gateway at `885d605e56` running with `--allow-unconfigured`.

**Terminal transcript**: `openclaw cron add` → `cron show` → `cron run` (manual, triggers error) → `cron show` (job still exists, enabled=true, nextRunAtMs preserved) → `cron list` (job present) → `cron runs` (run history with error status, nextRunAtMs unchanged).

| Step | Command | Result |
|------|---------|--------|
| Add job | `openclaw cron add --name proof-job --at ... --delete-after-run` | Created with `deleteAfterRun: true` |
| Manual run | `openclaw cron run <id>` | `{"ok": true, "enqueued": true}` |
| After run | `openclaw cron show <id>` | `enabled: true`, `nextRunAtMs: preserved` ✅ |
| Job exists | `openclaw cron list` | Job still listed ✅ |
| Run history | `openclaw cron runs --id <id>` | `status: "error"`, `nextRunAtMs: preserved` ✅ |


## Real CLI proof at head SHA `642c3450d9` (Docker Gateway, 2026-05-21)

**Environment**: Docker `node:24-bookworm-slim` (aarch64), patched Gateway built from PR head.

| Test | Command | Before (main) | After (PR head) |
|------|---------|---------------|-----------------|
| A: at-job deleteAfterRun | `cron run` → `cron show` | Job deleted/disabled | `enabled: true`, `nextRunAtMs` preserved ✅ |
| B: every-job manual error | `cron run` → `cron show` | `nextRunAtMs` rewritten by backoff | `nextRunAtMs` unchanged ✅ |
| A+B: counters | `cron runs` | `consecutiveErrors` mutated | Unchanged ✅ |

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` no longer consumes `deleteAfterRun` one-shot jobs, and no longer perturbs retry counters, at-job retry/disable state, recurring backoff, or failure-alert cooldown.
- **Real environment tested**: Docker container built from PR HEAD `3d32c9baff`, Linux aarch64 (OrbStack), Node.js `v24.14.0`, Vitest `v4.1.7`.
- **Failing proof before fix**: On upstream/main, `applyJobResult` has no manual-run discriminator — manual runs unconditionally drive `deleteAfterRun`, counter mutations, at-job disable, recurring backoff, and alert cooldown. The 12 regression tests added by this PR do not exist on main.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
# Build Docker image from PR HEAD
docker build --target build -t openclaw-pr83933:test .

# Test 1: Timer regression tests (includes all 12 manual-run isolation tests)
docker run --rm openclaw-pr83933:test node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts

# Test 2: Failure alert tests (adapted for manual-run isolation)
docker run --rm openclaw-pr83933:test node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts

# Test 3: Lint check
docker run --rm openclaw-pr83933:test npx oxlint src/cron/service/timer.ts src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts src/cron/service/ops.ts

# Test 4: Full cron test suite
docker run --rm openclaw-pr83933:test node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

Test 1 — Timer regression: **55 tests passed** (284ms)
```
 ✓ cron src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts (55 tests) 284ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  55 passed (55)
```

Test 2 — Failure alert: **10 tests passed** (63ms)
```
 ✓ cron src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts (10 tests) 63ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  10 passed (10)
```

Test 3 — Lint: **0 errors, 0 warnings**
```
Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.
Finished in 13ms on 4 files with 189 rules using 10 threads.
```

Test 4 — Full cron suite: **1000 tests passed** across 98 test files
```
 Test Files  1 failed | 97 passed (98)
      Tests  2 failed | 1000 passed (1002)
```
The 2 failures are in `cron-protocol-conformance.test.ts` — these look for Swift source files (`apps/macos/Sources/OpenClaw/CronModels.swift`) which are not present in the Docker build target. These failures are pre-existing and unrelated to this PR.

- **Observed result after fix**: All 12 new regression tests pass, confirming that manual runs preserve one-shot jobs (`deleteAfterRun`), do not increment/reset counters, do not disable at-jobs on error, do not rewrite recurring `nextRunAtMs` with backoff, and do not clear failure-alert cooldown. Control-group tests confirm scheduled runs still drive all 5 state effects normally.

- **What was not tested**: Live Gateway CLI `openclaw cron run` end-to-end (covered by earlier Docker CLI proof at `642c3450d9` — subsequent commits were formatting-only changes via oxfmt). The `cron-protocol-conformance` tests require macOS app source and cannot run in Docker.


## Real behavior proof (Docker, HEAD `6bc3b9e374`, 2026-05-30)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` no longer consumes `deleteAfterRun` one-shot jobs, and no longer perturbs retry counters, at-job retry/disable state, recurring backoff, failure-alert cooldown, or skipped-run alert counters.
- **Real environment tested**: Docker container built from PR HEAD `6bc3b9e374`, Linux aarch64 (OrbStack), Node.js `v24.14.0`, Vitest `v4.1.7`.
- **Failing proof before fix**: On upstream/main, `applyJobResult` has no manual-run discriminator — manual runs unconditionally drive all 5 scheduled-state effects plus skipped-run alert paths. The 14 regression tests added by this PR do not exist on main.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
# Build Docker image from PR HEAD
docker build --target build -t openclaw-pr83933:test .

# Test 1: Timer regression tests (all 14 manual-run isolation tests)
docker run --rm openclaw-pr83933:test node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts

# Test 2: Failure alert tests (adapted for manual-run isolation)
docker run --rm openclaw-pr83933:test node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts

# Test 3: Lint check
docker run --rm openclaw-pr83933:test npx oxlint src/cron/service/timer.ts src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts src/cron/service/ops.ts

# Test 4: Full cron test suite
docker run --rm openclaw-pr83933:test node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

Test 1 — Timer regression: **56 tests passed** (234ms)
```
 ✓ cron src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts (56 tests) 234ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  56 passed (56)
```

Test 2 — Failure alert: **11 tests passed** (92ms)
```
 ✓ cron src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts (11 tests) 92ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  11 passed (11)
```

Test 3 — Lint: **0 errors, 0 warnings**
```
Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.
Finished in 10ms on 4 files with 189 rules using 10 threads.
```

Test 4 — Full cron suite: **1002 tests passed** across 98 test files
```
 Test Files  1 failed | 97 passed (98)
      Tests  2 failed | 1002 passed (1004)
```
The 2 failures are in `cron-protocol-conformance.test.ts` — these look for Swift source files (`apps/macos/Sources/OpenClaw/CronModels.swift`) which are not present in the Docker build target. Pre-existing and unrelated to this PR.

- **Observed result after fix**: All 14 new regression tests pass, covering all 6 guarded state effects including the newly-added manual skipped-run isolation:
  - Manual `deleteAfterRun` at-job → NOT deleted ✅
  - Manual error → counters NOT incremented ✅
  - Manual success → counters NOT reset, cooldown NOT cleared ✅
  - Manual at-job error → NOT disabled, nextRunAtMs preserved ✅
  - Manual every-job error → backoff NOT applied, nextRunAtMs preserved ✅
  - Manual skipped at-job → consecutiveSkipped NOT incremented, alert NOT fired, all state preserved ✅
  - Manual skipped run with `includeSkipped: true` → failure alert NOT emitted ✅

  Control-group tests confirm scheduled runs still drive all state effects normally.

- **What was not tested**: Live Gateway CLI `openclaw cron run` end-to-end (covered by earlier Docker CLI proof at `642c3450d9` — subsequent commits were formatting + test coverage changes). The `cron-protocol-conformance` tests require macOS app source and cannot run in Docker.

### Manual-run state contract

`cron run` is a debug/verification command. Manual runs record their outcome (lastRunAtMs, lastStatus, lastError) but do not advance scheduled state. This makes manual runs safe, non-destructive operations that cannot interfere with scheduled execution. No reasonable user scenario depends on manual runs consuming one-shot jobs or driving retry/backoff state.

## Real behavior proof (Docker, HEAD `ccb89e3f11`, 2026-06-02)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `lastRunWasManual` field was missing from the `CronJobStateSchema` and `CronJobStatePatchSchema` in `packages/gateway-protocol/src/schema/cron.ts`. Both schemas use `additionalProperties: false`, so returning a cron job state containing `lastRunWasManual` would fail gateway protocol validation on `cron list`/`cron show`. Added `lastRunWasManual: Type.Optional(Type.Boolean())` to both schemas.

- **Real environment tested**: Docker container (`node:24-bookworm`), Linux aarch64 (OrbStack), Node.js `v24.16.0`, Vitest `v4.1.7`, patched OpenClaw at commit `ccb89e3f11`.

- **Failing proof before fix**: On the previous commit (`45efd1abdd`), `CronJobStateSchema` did not include `lastRunWasManual`. Any cron job state object with `lastRunWasManual: true` (set by `timer.ts:414` after a manual run) would be rejected by TypeBox validation due to `additionalProperties: false`. The `CronJobStatePatchSchema` was similarly missing the field, preventing state patches that include it.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
docker run --rm \
  -v "$(pwd):/app" \
  -v /app/node_modules \
  -e NODE_OPTIONS="--max-old-space-size=8192" \
  node:24-bookworm \
  bash -c '
    cd /app && npm install 2>&1 | tail -3 && \
    node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
      src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts \
      src/cron/service.restart-catchup.test.ts \
      src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts \
      src/cron/cron-protocol-conformance.test.ts \
      src/cron/cron-protocol-schema.test.ts 2>&1'
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```
 ✓ cron src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts (56 tests) 335ms
 ✓ cron src/cron/service.restart-catchup.test.ts (15 tests) 54ms
 ✓ cron src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts (10 tests) 31ms
 ✓ cron src/cron/cron-protocol-conformance.test.ts (3 tests) 3ms
 ✓ cron src/cron/cron-protocol-schema.test.ts (2 tests) 0ms

 Test Files  5 passed (5)
      Tests  86 passed (86)
   Start at  07:37:28
   Duration  3.44s (transform 2.31s, setup 280ms, import 2.62s, tests 424ms, environment 0ms)
```

- **Observed result after fix**: All 86 tests pass including:
  - `timer.regression.test.ts`: 56 tests — manual run sets `lastRunWasManual: true` on state (line 2531), state now passes schema validation.
  - `service.restart-catchup.test.ts`: 15 tests — restart catch-up checks `lastRunWasManual` in state fixture (line 224) and correctly re-runs the job.
  - `cron-protocol-conformance.test.ts`: 3 tests — schema conformance verified (delivery modes, status shape, failover reasons).
  - `cron-protocol-schema.test.ts`: 2 tests — schema structure validated (deprecated alias, failure notification state).

- **What was not tested**: Live `cron list`/`cron show` gateway roundtrip with the patched schema. The schema fix is structural (adding a field to a TypeBox object with `additionalProperties: false`) and the test suite validates that state objects with `lastRunWasManual` are accepted by the schema.

## Live Gateway CLI roundtrip proof (Docker, HEAD `ccb89e3f11`)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` on a `deleteAfterRun` at-job must not delete the job, must not disable it, must not clear `nextRunAtMs`, and must set `lastRunWasManual: true` in state. The `lastRunWasManual` field must pass `CronJobStateSchema` validation (which uses `additionalProperties: false`).
- **Real environment tested**: Docker container `node:24-bookworm` (linux/arm64, OrbStack), Node.js `v24.16.0`, OpenClaw Gateway built from source at commit `ccb89e3f11`.
- **Steps**:
  1. Built OpenClaw from source on the PR branch
  2. Started Gateway inside Docker with `gateway run --dev --allow-unconfigured --auth token`
  3. Created a `deleteAfterRun` at-job via `cron add`
  4. Manually ran the job via `cron run` (resulted in error due to missing codex-linux-arm64 in Docker — this is the interesting case: manual error on a deleteAfterRun job)
  5. Verified job survival, `lastRunWasManual: true`, `enabled: true`, and `nextRunAtMs` preserved via `cron show` and `cron list`
  6. Verified `cron list` and `cron show` returned the `lastRunWasManual` field without schema validation errors

<details>


- **Observed result**: After manual `cron run` (which triggered an error due to missing codex dependency in Docker):
  - Job **survived** — still present in `cron list` and `cron show` ✅

  - `lastRunWasManual: true` — new field present and accepted by schema ✅
  - No `consecutiveErrors` field — manual error did NOT increment counter ✅
  - Run history shows `runId: "manual:..."` prefix confirming manual execution path ✅
  - Both `cron show` and `cron list` returned `lastRunWasManual` without schema validation errors, proving `CronJobStateSchema` correctly includes the field ✅

- **What was not tested**: Manual run with `status: "ok"` (would require a working LLM provider in Docker). The `shouldDelete` guard path (`wouldDelete && !opts?.isManual`) is exercised by unit tests. Multi-node cluster, production load.


## Real behavior proof (Docker, HEAD `8596607fca`, 2026-06-02 — backoff fix)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` on a recurring job that errors must not open a backoff window that blocks the next scheduled fire. Previously, `isErrorBackoffPending()`, `resolveJobErrorBackoffUntilMs()`, `isPendingErrorBackoffSlot()`, and `recomputeJobNextRunAtMs()` read `lastRunStatus` and `lastRunAtMs` without checking `lastRunWasManual`, causing a manual error to push `nextRunAtMs` forward and block the timer-scheduled fire.

- **Real environment tested**: Docker container `node:24-bookworm` (linux/arm64), Node.js `v24.16.0`, Vitest `v4.1.7`, patched OpenClaw at commit `8596607fca`.

- **Failing proof before fix**: On upstream/main, `resolveJobErrorBackoffUntilMs()` does not check `lastRunWasManual`. A recurring every-job with `lastRunWasManual: true` and `lastRunStatus: "error"` would have `resolveJobErrorBackoffUntilMs()` return a backoff deadline, `isErrorBackoffPending()` would return `true`, and `isRunnableJob()` would return `false` — blocking the next scheduled fire. `recomputeJobNextRunAtMs()` would also push `nextRunAtMs` forward to the backoff floor. Test file authored as part of this PR.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
# Unit tests in Docker
docker run --rm --platform linux/arm64 \
  -v "$(pwd):/app" -w /app \
  -e NODE_OPTIONS="--max-old-space-size=8192" -e CI=true \
  node:24-bookworm bash -c \
  "node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
    src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts \
    src/cron/service.restart-catchup.test.ts \
    src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts"

# Live Gateway CLI in Docker
docker run --rm --platform linux/arm64 \
  -v "$(pwd):/app" -w /app \
  -e NODE_OPTIONS="--max-old-space-size=8192" -e CI=true \
  -e OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-test \
  -e OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN=test-token-123 \
  node:24-bookworm bash -c '
    mkdir -p /tmp/openclaw-test
    echo "{\"gateway\":{\"port\":19001,\"bind\":\"loopback\"}}" > /tmp/openclaw-test/openclaw.json
    node openclaw.mjs gateway run --dev --allow-unconfigured --token test-token-123 --port 19001 &
    sleep 8
    node openclaw.mjs cron add --name test-at-delete --at "2026-06-02T23:00:00Z" --message reminder --delete-after-run --url ws://127.0.0.1:19001 --token test-token-123
    # extract ID, then: cron run <ID>, cron show <ID>, cron list
    node openclaw.mjs cron add --name test-recurring --every 10s --message "recurring check" --url ws://127.0.0.1:19001 --token test-token-123
    # extract ID, then: cron run <ID>, cron show <ID>
    kill %1'
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

<details>
<summary>Docker unit test output (82 tests pass)</summary>

```
 ✓ cron src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts (57 tests) 254ms
 ✓ cron src/cron/service.restart-catchup.test.ts (15 tests) 44ms
 ✓ cron src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts (10 tests) 25ms

 Test Files  3 passed (3)
      Tests  82 passed (82)
   Start at  11:08:37
   Duration  2.77s (transform 1.83s, setup 250ms, import 2.09s, tests 323ms, environment 0ms)

[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 5.85s
```

</details>

<details>
<summary>Docker Gateway CLI output</summary>

```
=== Node.js version ===
v24.16.0
=== Starting Gateway ===
[gateway] ready

TEST 1: Create deleteAfterRun at-job
  → id: dbfbe16b-c44a-4aa1-b820-889dbdbcb9a2, deleteAfterRun: true, nextRunAtMs: 1780441200000

TEST 2: Manual run on deleteAfterRun at-job
  → { "ok": true, "enqueued": true, "runId": "manual:dbfbe16b-..." }

TEST 3: Verify at-job survived manual run
  → id: dbfbe16b-..., enabled: true, deleteAfterRun: true, nextRunAtMs: 1780441200000 (PRESERVED)
  → status: "running" (agent executing but will error — no LLM provider in Docker)

TEST 4: Create recurring every-10s job
  → id: 18b2583a-7990-4667-95cd-591614c858f9, everyMs: 10000, nextRunAtMs: 1780398505454

TEST 5: Manual run on recurring job
  → { "ok": true, "enqueued": true, "runId": "manual:18b2583a-..." }

TEST 6: Verify recurring job state after manual run
  → id: 18b2583a-..., enabled: true, nextRunAtMs: 1780398505454 (PRESERVED, NOT pushed by backoff)

TEST 7: Schema validation — cron list --json
  → Both jobs returned with valid schema, no validation errors
  → total: 2, both enabled, both with preserved nextRunAtMs

Gateway stopped.
```

</details>

- **Observed result after fix**:
  1. **deleteAfterRun at-job manual run**: Job survived manual run, `enabled: true`, `deleteAfterRun: true`, `nextRunAtMs` preserved ✅
  2. **Recurring job manual run**: Job's `nextRunAtMs` preserved at natural cadence, NOT pushed forward by backoff ✅
  3. **Schema validation**: Both `cron list --json` and `cron show --json` returned `lastRunWasManual` field without validation errors ✅
  4. **New regression test**: `#83933: manual error on recurring job does not block scheduled fire via backoff` — verifies `resolveJobErrorBackoffUntilMs()` returns `undefined` for manual errors, `isRunnableJob()` returns `true` within theoretical backoff window, `recomputeNextRunsForMaintenance()` computes natural cadence (not backoff floor), and control case confirms scheduled errors still apply backoff ✅

- **What was not tested**: Manual run with `status: "ok"` on recurring job (requires working LLM in Docker). Multi-node cluster, production load, actual delivery to chat channels.

commands.author: SunnyShu0925

target.title: fix(memory-core): harden dreaming daily-file writes and drain dangling recall refs
target.body:
## Summary

Memory-core dreaming daily-file writes could truncate files on crash because `fs.writeFile` is non-atomic. Additionally, the recall store accumulates dangling references when daily memory files are silently deleted, wasting promotion cycles on dead entries. This PR hardens the write path with atomic rename and drains dangling recall references during repair.

- **Problem**: (1) `writeDailyDreamingPhaseBlock` and `writeDeepDreamingReport` use `fs.writeFile` (non-atomic on Linux), so a process crash mid-write can truncate daily memory or dreaming report files. (2) When daily memory files disappear (as in #84882), recall entries with dangling source paths are never cleaned up — the promotion pipeline wastes cycles attempting to rehydrate them and silently fails. (3) ClawSweeper P1: manual `tmp+randomUUID+rename` loses file permissions — a new file gets the default umask mode instead of preserving the existing file's mode. (4) ClawSweeper P1: dangling-ref pruning catch block silently swallows `EACCES`/`EPERM`, treating permission-denied files as missing and incorrectly deleting valid recall entries.
- **Solution**: (1) Use `replaceFileAtomic` from `@openclaw/fs-safe` for all three dreaming write paths — it atomically writes via temp+rename and supports `preserveExistingMode`. (2) In `repairShortTermPromotionArtifacts`, detect entries whose source file no longer exists on disk and remove them, surfacing the count in the log summary. (3) Replace manual `tmp-${randomUUID()}+writeFile+rename` with `replaceFileAtomic({ preserveExistingMode: true })` for inline daily writes, preserving the existing file's permission bits. (4) Narrow the `fs.access` catch block to only suppress `ENOENT` — `EACCES`/`EPERM` now rethrow instead of being treated as "missing."
- **What changed**:
  - `extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-markdown.ts`: manual temp+rename replaced with `replaceFileAtomic` (inline path uses `preserveExistingMode: true`; separate/deep paths use explicit `mode: 0o600`); removed `randomUUID` import
  - `extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming.ts`: `formatRepairSummary` includes dangling count
  - `extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.ts`: `RepairShortTermPromotionArtifactsResult` gains `removedDanglingRefs`; repair step drains entries with missing source files; `fs.access` catch narrowed to `ENOENT`-only; audit exposes `recall-store-dangling-ref` issue code with `danglingRefCount`
  - `extensions/memory-core/src/cli.runtime.ts`: show dangling-refs in CLI repair summary
  - `src/plugin-sdk/memory-core-engine-runtime.ts`: `ShortTermAuditSummary` gains `danglingRefCount`; `RepairShortTermPromotionArtifactsResult` gains `removedDanglingRefs`; `ShortTermAuditIssue.code` union includes `recall-store-dangling-ref`
  - `extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-markdown.test.ts`: content-preservation and orphan-tmp-file tests
  - `extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.test.ts`: dangling-reference removal and audit issue tests
- **What did NOT change**: Dreaming pipeline orchestration, recall store schema, promotion candidate ranking, markdown block replacement logic, ingestion/sweep phases

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related to #84882
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

Issue #84882 reports that memory-core Dreaming silently deletes daily memory files (`memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md`) — 46 files vanished in one night. After extensive source review, the exact deletion mechanism remains unknown (ClawSweeper confirmed the same). This PR takes a defense-in-depth approach: atomic writes prevent crash-induced truncation, and dangling-reference cleanup prevents the recall store from accumulating dead entries that waste promotion cycles.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior addressed**: (1) Dreaming managed-block writes to daily memory files use non-atomic `fs.writeFile` — a crash mid-write can leave the target file empty or truncated. (2) When daily memory files are silently deleted (as in #84882), recall entries with dangling source paths remain in the store forever, causing `rehydratePromotionCandidate` to silently return null for every attempt. (3) ClawSweeper review flagged `fs.writeFile(tmpPath) + fs.rename(tmpPath, target)` as a file-permission regression — the temp file inherits the umask (typically 0o644) instead of the existing file's mode (e.g. 0o600). (4) ClawSweeper review flagged the `catch {}` block in `repairShortTermPromotionArtifacts` as treating `EACCES`/`EPERM` identically to `ENOENT`, meaning a permission-denied daily memory file would cause its recall entry to be pruned as if the file were actually gone.

- **Real environment tested**: Linux x64, Node 22.19.0, branch `fix/memory-dreaming-daily-delete-84882`

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
node --import tsx extensions/memory-core/tmp-proof/proof-94537.mts
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```console
$ node --import tsx extensions/memory-core/tmp-proof/proof-94537.mts

========================================================================
PROOF 1: replaceFileAtomic preserves existing file mode
========================================================================
  Original mode: 600
  After: 600  ✓ PRESERVED (was 600)

========================================================================
PROOF 2: replaceFileAtomic on new file (separate report path)
========================================================================
  New file mode: 600  ✓ EXPECTED 600

========================================================================
PROOF 3: No temp file orphans after atomic writes
========================================================================
  Temp orphans: 0  ✓ CLEAN

========================================================================
PROOF 4: writeDailyDreamingPhaseBlock (production dreaming code path)
========================================================================
  User content outside dream block: ✓ INTACT
  Dreaming block written:           ✓ YES
  File mode:                        600

========================================================================
PROOF 5: dangling recall reference cleanup during repair
========================================================================
  Entries before repair: 2 (healthy_entry + dangling_entry)
  Dangling present before: ✓ YES (expected)

  repairShortTermPromotionArtifacts result:
    changed:              true
    removedDanglingRefs:  1  ✓ ONE DANGLING REMOVED
    removedInvalidEntries: 0
    removedOverflowEntries: 0
    rewroteStore:          true

  Entries after repair:  1
  Dangling entry removed: ✓ YES
  Healthy entry kept:     ✓ YES

========================================================================
PROOF 6: narrowed catch block (EACCES/EPERM rethrow)
========================================================================
  ✓ Catch block at line 1340 only suppresses ENOENT
          }
        } catch (err) {
          if ((err as NodeJS.ErrnoException).code === "ENOENT") {
            continue;
          }

========================================================================
SUMMARY
========================================================================
  P1-1: replaceFileAtomic mode preservation:   ✓ PASS
  P1-2: replaceFileAtomic new file mode (600): ✓ PASS
  P1-3: No temp file orphans:                  ✓ PASS
  P1-4: Dreaming inline write (prod code):     ✓ PASS
  P1-5: Dangling ref cleanup (repair):        ✓ PASS
  P1-6: Narrowed catch (ENOENT only):         ✓ PASS
  All real behavior proofs complete.
```

- **Observed result after fix**:
  1. All 6 real behavior proofs pass — each exercises actual production code paths, not mocks
  2. `replaceFileAtomic({ preserveExistingMode: true })` keeps existing file mode (0o600) — method: `rename`
  3. New file gets explicit mode 0o600 for separate report path — method: `rename`
  4. Zero temp file orphans left after atomic writes
  5. `writeDailyDreamingPhaseBlock` preserves user content outside managed blocks while writing dream block atomically
  6. `repairShortTermPromotionArtifacts`: 1 dangling entry removed, healthy entry kept, store rewritten
  7. Narrowed catch block at `short-term-promotion.ts:1340` only suppresses `ENOENT` — `EACCES`/`EPERM` propagate instead of triggering false dangling-ref pruning

- **What was not tested**:
  - Live dreaming cron trigger against a real OpenClaw instance (requires running gateway with dreaming enabled)
  - Multi-workspace dreaming scenario
  - EACCES/EPERM scenario on a restricted filesystem
  - macOS or Windows (tmp-file semantics and permissions differ)

## Root Cause (if applicable)

The exact deletion mechanism for daily memory files in #84882 remains **unknown** after source review (ClawSweeper confirmed same). The recall-store normalization step (`repairShortTermPromotionArtifacts`) is temporally correlated with the deletions but only operates on the SQLite recall store — it does not touch daily memory files. The dreaming pipeline's only write path to daily files (`writeDailyDreamingPhaseBlock`) operates exclusively on the current day's file.

**Provenance**:
- introduced by: unknown (exact deletion path not found)
- made visible by: dreaming pipeline "normalized recall artifacts before dreaming" step (`dreaming.ts:588-590`) — temporal correlation only
- Confidence: unknown

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Dreaming log line now includes dangling count when recall entries reference deleted files: `"normalized recall artifacts before dreaming (rewrote recall store (-2 dangling))"`
- Dreaming repair silently removes recall entries whose source files are gone (previously these were kept indefinitely and silently failed during promotion)

## Security Impact (required)

- [x] New permissions/capabilities? No — `replaceFileAtomic` is a standard library function from the existing `@openclaw/fs-safe` dependency
- [x] Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- [x] New/changed network calls? No
- [x] Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- [x] Data access scope changed? No

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios**: `replaceFileAtomic` mode preservation (0o600 → 0o600), new file mode (0o600), zero tmp orphans, `writeDailyDreamingPhaseBlock` user content preservation, dangling ref removal via `repairShortTermPromotionArtifacts`, narrowed ENOENT-only catch block (oxlint zero errors)
- **Edge cases checked**: empty original file, file with multiple managed blocks, file with user content before/after/between blocks, separate storage mode, existing file mode preservation vs. new file mode assignment, repair with mixed healthy+dangling entries
- **What you did NOT verify**: Live dreaming cron trigger, multi-workspace dreaming, EACCES/EPERM on restricted filesystem, macOS/Windows

## Compatibility / Migration

- [x] Backward compatible? Yes — no config or API surface changes; new fields in `RepairShortTermPromotionArtifactsResult` and `ShortTermAuditSummary` are additive (consumers that destructure ignore unknown fields)
- [x] Config/env changes? No
- [x] Migration needed? No

## Best-fix Verdict

- **Best fix**: Yes. Defense-in-depth is the right approach when the exact deletion mechanism is unknown. Using `replaceFileAtomic` from the existing `@openclaw/fs-safe` dependency is strictly better than ad-hoc `tmp-${randomUUID()}+writeFile+rename` — it provides guaranteed atomicity, mode preservation via `preserveExistingMode`, and configurable temp-prefix naming. Narrowing the `catch {}` block to `ENOENT`-only follows the established project pattern (e.g. `dreaming-markdown.ts` already uses `if (err.code === "ENOENT")` for the same reason). Both are ClawSweeper-confirmed best practices for filesystem hardening.
- **Refactor needed**: No. The changes are scoped and minimal.
- **Alternatives considered**: (1) Keeping `tmp-${randomUUID()}+rename` with explicit `chmod` after rename — rejected because it introduces a TOCTOU window between rename and chmod where the wrong mode is live. (2) Broad `catch {}` swallowing EACCES/EPERM with a warning log — rejected because it could still cause data loss (valid entries silently pruned) and the permission issue is better surfaced to the application layer.

## AI Assistance

- **AI-assisted**: Yes
- **Co-Authored-By**: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>
- **Human confirmed understanding of code changes**: Yes

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Highest risk**: The dangling-reference detection does an `fs.access` call per recall entry during repair. On very large recall stores (512 entries max), this is bounded but still O(n) filesystem calls. **Mitigation**: The repair step runs only once per dreaming cycle (typically nightly), so overhead is amortized. Only entries with `source === "memory"` are checked.

- **SDK facade compatibility**: `ShortTermAuditSummary` gains `danglingRefCount`, `ShortTermAuditIssue.code` union includes `recall-store-dangling-ref`. **Mitigation**: These are additive type changes — existing consumers that ignore unknown fields continue to work. The `ShortTermAuditIssue.code` union extension follows the same pattern as the existing `recall-store-overflow` code added in prior PRs.

- **Crash safety**: `replaceFileAtomic` handles temp file creation and rename internally with configurable temp prefixes (`dreaming-inline`, `dreaming-report`, `dreaming-deep`). If the write succeeds but the rename fails, a `dreaming-*` orphan may remain — this is inherent to temp+rename atomicity and is strictly safer than `fs.writeFile` overwrite (which could truncate the target file). The proof script confirms zero orphan temp files under normal operation.

---
Related to #84882

target.title: feat(config): support SecretRef for phone numbers in channel configs
target.body:
## Summary

Phone numbers are PII. If you're putting them in `allowFrom`, `defaultTo`, or `groupAllowFrom`, they're sitting in plaintext config. This PR adds SecretRef support for phone number fields across all messaging channels so those values can live in a secret provider instead.

- **Problem:** Phone numbers in channel configs (`allowFrom`, `defaultTo`, `groupAllowFrom`, `account`, `reactionAllowlist`) only accepted plain strings — no way to keep them out of plaintext config files.
- **Why it matters:** Phone numbers are PII under GDPR/CCPA. SecretRef support lets users store them in env vars, files, or exec-based secret providers.
- **What changed:** WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, BlueBubbles, and voice-call channels now accept `SecretRef` objects for all phone number / sender identity fields. Exported TypeScript types, Zod schemas, secret contracts, runtime resolution, config UI hints, docs, and tests are all updated.
- **What did NOT change:** Existing plain string configs continue to work. No breaking changes to the config schema.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #56502
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A — this is a new feature, not a bug fix.

- Root cause: N/A
- Missing detection / guardrail: N/A
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A — this is a new feature. Tests are added for coverage, not regression.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `config-schema.test.ts` and `secret-contract.test.ts` for each channel, `zod-schema.secretref-allowfrom.test.ts`, `target-registry.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: SecretRef objects are accepted in phone number fields; invalid SecretRef shapes are rejected; `normalizeAllowFrom` filters out unresolved SecretRef objects instead of stringifying them to `[object Object]`; secret target registry discovers plugin manifest `secretInputs.paths`
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Schema-level tests catch accept/reject at the boundary; the `normalizeAllowFrom` test catches the runtime filtering; the target registry test catches manifest-driven secret discovery
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A — new feature surface
- If no new test is added, why not: Tests are added

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- `allowFrom`, `defaultTo`, `groupAllowFrom` (and Signal's `account`, `reactionAllowlist`) now accept SecretRef objects in addition to plain strings across WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and BlueBubbles.
- Voice-call's `fromNumber`, `toNumber`, and `allowFrom` accept SecretRef objects.
- Config UI hints mark these fields as `sensitive: true` so UIs mask them.

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? Yes — phone number fields can now be stored as SecretRefs
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: SecretRefs are resolved through the existing secret provider pipeline. No new secret resolution paths. `normalizeAllowFrom` filters out unresolved SecretRef objects so they can't leak as `[object Object]` strings into allowlists.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux (Devin VM)
- Runtime/container: Node 22, pnpm
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, BlueBubbles, voice-call
- Relevant config (redacted): `"allowFrom": [{ "source": "env", "provider": "default", "id": "MY_PHONE" }]`

### Steps

1. Add a SecretRef to a phone number field in config (e.g. `"allowFrom": [{ "source": "env", "provider": "default", "id": "MY_PHONE" }]`)
2. Run `openclaw doctor` or start the gateway
3. The SecretRef is resolved through the configured secret provider

### Expected

- Config parses successfully with SecretRef objects in phone number fields
- Plain strings continue to work as before

### Actual

- Confirmed via unit tests (73 tests across 8 test files, all passing)

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

All tests pass: `pnpm tsgo` (typecheck), `pnpm check` (lint), `pnpm test` (73 relevant tests across schema validation, secret contracts, runtime normalization, and target registry).

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Schema acceptance/rejection for all 5 channels, `normalizeAllowFrom` SecretRef filtering, secret target registry manifest discovery, voice-call manifest path merging after rebase
- Edge cases checked: Invalid SecretRef shapes rejected, mixed arrays of strings + SecretRefs + numbers handled correctly, undefined input returns empty array, objects with custom `toString()` still work in `normalizeStringEntries`
- What I did **not** verify: End-to-end with a running gateway and real secret provider

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No (additive only — existing configs unchanged)
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Unresolved SecretRef objects could reach `normalizeAllowFrom` at runtime
  - Mitigation: `normalizeAllowFrom` now filters out SecretRef objects via `isSecretRef()` before string normalization, preventing `[object Object]` entries in allowlists

---

**AI models used in this branch:**

GPT-5 (OpenAI Codex) via Codex
Claude Sonnet 4.6 (Anthropic) via Devin

target.title: fix(media): allow private openai compatible audio transcription endpoints
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Audio media-understanding transcription could target self-hosted OpenAI-compatible endpoints, but audio config did not expose the private-network request opt-in used by guarded provider HTTP.
- Why it matters: Trusted local, RFC1918, or tailnet Whisper-style endpoints need explicit private-network access without globally relaxing media request SSRF policy.
- What changed: Added audio-only request config support for `tools.media.audio.request.allowPrivateNetwork`, aligned schema/generated metadata/docs, threaded audio request overrides into provider execution, and aligned OpenAI-compatible loopback audio auth with the chat-completion local auth pattern by using the shared `custom-local` marker only for loopback OpenAI-compatible audio bases.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): Image and video media request config still reject `allowPrivateNetwork`, private-network access remains disabled by default, explicit `request.auth` still injects the configured auth header, and media/audio does not add the Ollama-specific local-auth branch.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes N/A
- Related N/A
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Audio media config reused the generic media request schema/type path, which rejected `allowPrivateNetwork`, while provider execution needed an audio-specific trusted request override path. The local no-auth path also needed to follow the existing chat-completion pattern: synthesize the shared `custom-local` marker for loopback OpenAI-compatible endpoints, then avoid sending provider-default auth for that marker.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Existing schema and runner tests did not cover audio-specific private-network request overrides or loopback OpenAI-compatible audio endpoints without provider auth.
- Contributing context (if known): The config docs baseline and audio field metadata are generated/aligned surfaces for this config contract.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/config/schema.test.ts`, `src/media-understanding/openai-compatible-audio.test.ts`, `src/media-understanding/runner.proxy.test.ts`, `src/media-understanding/shared.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Audio config accepts and forwards `allowPrivateNetwork`, image/video reject it, per-model audio request config works, loopback OpenAI-compatible audio can synthesize `custom-local` when provider auth is absent, synthetic local audio auth sends no provider-default `Authorization`, explicit request auth still wins, and Ollama does not get the media/audio local-auth branch.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: These tests exercise the config contract and provider request assembly without needing a live transcription service.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Users can opt audio transcription requests into private-network endpoints with `tools.media.audio.request.allowPrivateNetwork` and per-model audio request config. Loopback OpenAI-compatible audio endpoints such as `http://127.0.0.1:8000/v1` can run without provider auth, matching local OpenAI-compatible chat-completion providers. The OpenAI provider docs now show the loopback example base URL.

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: The new network capability is an explicit audio-only opt-in for trusted private endpoints; defaults remain blocked by the SSRF guard and image/video still reject the private-network flag. Local no-auth audio is restricted to loopback OpenAI-compatible audio providers using the existing non-secret `custom-local` marker. Explicit request auth still injects the configured header when present.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux 6.1.0-44-cloud-amd64 x86_64
- Runtime/container: Node v22.22.2, pnpm 10.33.0
- Model/provider: OpenAI-compatible audio transcription config path
- Integration/channel (if any): Media understanding audio attachments
- Relevant config (redacted): `tools.media.audio.request.allowPrivateNetwork`, `tools.media.audio.request.auth`, `tools.media.audio.models[].baseUrl`

### Steps

1. Parse audio, image, and video media config shapes through the runtime zod schema.
2. Run audio provider execution with provider-level, audio-level, and per-model request overrides.
3. Run provider HTTP request assembly with synthetic local auth and explicit request auth.

### Expected

- Audio request config accepts and forwards private-network opt-in only when explicitly configured.
- Image/video reject `allowPrivateNetwork` under media request config.
- Loopback OpenAI-compatible audio can use the shared synthetic local auth marker when provider auth is absent.
- Synthetic local auth does not become a provider-default `Authorization` header.
- Explicit request auth overrides synthetic local auth and injects the configured header.
- Ollama does not receive a media/audio local-auth special case.

### Actual

- Matches expected in local tests and changed-gate validation.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Local validation:

- `pnpm docs:list`
- `pnpm test src/media-understanding/openai-compatible-audio.test.ts src/media-understanding/runner.proxy.test.ts src/media-understanding/shared.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/model-auth.ts src/media-understanding/runner.entries.ts src/media-understanding/runner.proxy.test.ts src/media-understanding/runner.test-mocks.ts docs/providers/openai.md docs/nodes/audio.md CHANGELOG.md src/media-understanding/openai-compatible-audio.ts src/media-understanding/openai-compatible-audio.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm check:changed`

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

Set up a (WhisperLive)[https://github.com/collabora/WhisperLive] server locally. Modified openclaw.json to point to it with `media.audio.request.allowPrivateNetwork = true`:

```
"media": {
      "audio": {
        "enabled": true,
        "language": "en",
        "models": [
          {
            "provider": "openai",
            "model": "whisper-1",
            "baseUrl": "http://127.0.0.1:8000/v1",
          }
        ],
        "request": {
          "allowPrivateNetwork": true,
        }
      }
```

Verified audio transcription:

```
$ openclaw capability audio transcribe --file tmp/media-test/transcription-test.wav --language en --json
{
  "ok": true,
  "capability": "audio.transcribe",
  "transport": "local",
  "attempts": [],
  "outputs": [
    {
      "path": "/home/openclaw/.openclaw/workspace/tmp/media-test/transcription-test.wav",
      "text": "Open Claw Media Transcription Test. The passphrase is Brass Lobster 7.",
      "kind": "audio.transcription"
    }
  ]
}
```



## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

No PR review conversations currently exist.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes, optional new audio request config only
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Users could point audio transcription at private endpoints they do not fully trust.
  - Mitigation: The private-network exception is explicit, audio-only, disabled by default, documented as trusted-endpoint-only, and covered by schema/runtime tests.
- Risk: Synthetic local auth could accidentally be sent as provider-default auth.
  - Mitigation: OpenAI-compatible audio now uses the shared chat-completion local auth marker and omits provider-default auth for that marker while preserving explicit request auth overrides.

target.title: fix(exec): decode Windows command output with codepage-aware streaming
target.body:
## Summary
- Fix Windows exec output mojibake for CJK locales by decoding captured command output with the active Windows code page instead of assuming UTF-8.
- Preserve decoder state across stdout/stderr chunks or decode buffered byte streams so split GBK/Shift_JIS characters are not replaced.
- Add focused regression coverage for CP936/GBK Chinese output and split multibyte chunks.

## Credit
This carries forward the useful approach from @slepybear's source PR https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/64661, which could not be safely updated directly because the branch is closed, maintainer_can_modify=false, checks failed, and bot review identified unresolved decoder-state issues.

## Linked Issues
Fixes/addresses the Windows CJK exec mojibake family reported in #40613, #50519, and #56462.

## Validation
- pnpm -s vitest run src/infra/windows-encoding.test.ts src/process/exec.windows.test.ts
- pnpm check:changed

## Preflight Note

ProjectClownfish replacement details:

target.title: fix: skip setup-only channel plugins in outbound resolution and drain pending deliveries on qqbot reconnect
target.body:
## Summary

When the qqbot WebSocket reconnects after a session timeout (close code `4009`), `resolveOutboundChannelPlugin()` returns a setup-only plugin stub that lacks any send capability. This causes the `"Outbound not configured for channel: qqbot"` error, and all messages queued during the disconnection window are permanently lost.

## Root Cause

`resolveOutboundChannelPlugin()` resolves the first matching plugin entry for a channel id without verifying that the entry actually has send capability (`outbound.sendText`, `message.send.text`, etc.). During qqbot WebSocket reconnection, a setup-only plugin entry (registered for configuration/pairing but not for message delivery) shadows the real sending plugin, causing the resolution to short-circuit with a non-functional stub.

Regression introduced in 2026.5.28 (commit `e932160`).

## Fix

### 1. Split outbound channel resolution into shared and delivery paths (`channel-resolution.ts`)

Add a `channelPluginCanSend()` predicate that checks for any of the 10 known send methods across both the legacy `outbound` adapter and the newer `message.send` adapter:

- `outbound.sendText / sendMedia / sendPayload / sendPoll / sendFormattedText / sendFormattedMedia`
- `message.send.text / media / payload / poll`

Instead of gating the shared `resolveOutboundChannelPlugin()` resolver (which would break action-only channel plugins that have `actions.handleAction` but no send capability), a new **`resolveOutboundChannelPluginForDelivery()`** resolver applies the send-capability guard exclusively for message delivery paths.

- **`resolveOutboundChannelPlugin()`**: Shared resolver — returns any matching plugin regardless of send capability, preserving resolution for action dispatch, target resolution, and other non-send callers.
- **`resolveOutboundChannelPluginForDelivery()`**: Delivery resolver — skips setup-only entries and only returns plugins that can actually send messages.
- **`resolveOutboundChannelMessageAdapter()`**: Updated to use the delivery resolver.
- **`message.ts`**: `sendMessage`/`sendPoll` updated to use the delivery resolver.

### 2. Drain pending deliveries on qqbot reconnect (`extensions/qqbot/src/channel.ts`)

The `onReady` and `onResumed` gateway callbacks now call `drainPendingDeliveries()` from the plugin SDK delivery queue runtime, recovering messages that were queued while the WebSocket was disconnected. The drain is:

- Filtered by channel (`qqbot`) and account id, with a fallback for default-account entries where `accountId` may be `undefined`
- Deduplicated via `drainKey` to prevent concurrent drains
- Wrapped in try/catch so drain failures do not break the reconnection flow

## Tests

- `channel-resolution.test.ts`: 17 tests (5 new — setup-only plugin returns from shared resolver, action-only plugin resolves from shared but not delivery resolver, delivery resolver skips setup-only and returns sending fallback, legacy outbound adapter, poll-only outbound)
- `channel.test.ts`: 2 tests (new file — onReady drain, onResumed drain)

Fixes #88955.


## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: After a QQ Bot WebSocket disconnect (session takeover / close code 4009), `resolveOutboundChannelPlugin()` returned a setup-only plugin stub instead of the runtime entry, causing `"Outbound not configured for channel: qqbot"` and dropping all pending deliveries.

- **Real environment tested**: Local patched OpenClaw `2026.6.2` (commit `b90683e`, branch `fix/qqbot-reconnect-outbound-88955`), macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0. QQ Bot plugin `@openclaw/qqbot@2026.5.31-beta.4` with live QQ Bot credentials (appId `<redacted>`).

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. Built patched OpenClaw from fix branch: `pnpm build && npm pack --ignore-scripts`
  2. Installed globally: `npm install -g openclaw-2026.6.2.tgz` — confirmed version: `OpenClaw 2026.6.2 (b90683e)`
  3. Restarted gateway: `openclaw gateway restart`
  4. Observed qqbot plugin startup, WebSocket connection, and `Gateway ready` callback sequence in logs
  5. Verified normal QQ Bot C2C message round-trip: user sent "hello" → bot received → processed → replied with markdown (20 chars, 200 OK)
  6. Scanned full gateway log for `"Outbound not configured"` errors — zero matches
  7. Repeated restart cycle to confirm reconnect stability across multiple `Gateway ready` events

- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```
  # Patched OpenClaw installed and running
  $ openclaw --version
  OpenClaw 2026.6.2 (b90683e)

  # Gateway restart sequence — qqbot reconnects cleanly
  2026-06-02T22:38:53.727+08:00 [gateway] received SIGTERM; restarting
  2026-06-02T22:39:15.986+08:00 [gateway] http server listening (8 plugins: ..., qqbot, ...; 1.6s)
  2026-06-02T22:39:16.171+08:00 [qqbot] [qqbot:default] Starting gateway — appId=<redacted>, enabled=true
  2026-06-02T22:39:17.387+08:00 [qqbot] [default] Connecting to wss://api.sgroup.qq.com/websocket
  2026-06-02T22:39:18.468+08:00 [qqbot] [default] WebSocket connected
  2026-06-02T22:39:18.641+08:00 [qqbot] [qqbot:default] Gateway ready

  # Successful QQ Bot message delivery after reconnect
  2026-06-02T22:32:40.229+08:00 [qqbot] [default] Processing message from <redacted>: hello
  2026-06-02T22:32:59.431+08:00 [qqbot] [default] [qqbot:api] <<< Status: 200 OK
  2026-06-02T22:32:59.432+08:00 [qqbot] [default] Sent markdown chunk (20/20 chars) with 0 HTTP images (c2c)

  # Zero outbound resolution errors across entire log history
  $ grep -c "Outbound not configured.*qqbot" ~/Library/Logs/openclaw/gateway.log
  0

  # Multiple Gateway ready events over 4+ hours — all clean reconnects
  $ grep "qqbot.*Gateway ready" ~/Library/Logs/openclaw/gateway.log | wc -l
  18
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: The delivery resolver correctly skipped the setup-only plugin entry (which has no `sendText`/`sendMedia` methods) and resolved the runtime entry with full send capability. Outbound messages delivered successfully through the QQ Bot API (HTTP 200). Zero occurrences of the `"Outbound not configured for channel: qqbot"` error across 18 reconnect cycles over a 4+ hour observation window. The shared resolver continued to return all plugin entries for non-send callers (action dispatch, target resolution), preserving action-only plugin compatibility.

- **What was not tested**:

  Live drain proof is not reproducible on the fixed build. The `drainReconnectPendingDeliveries()` mechanism (Fix 2) was verified via unit tests but not observed draining actual queued entries in a live environment. This is not an oversight — it is a structural consequence of the resolver fix (Fix 1) eliminating the only deterministic cause of pending qqbot entries. Code-level analysis confirms this:

  **How the delivery queue works** (`deliver.ts:1254-1280`): Every outbound delivery is write-ahead enqueued via `enqueueDelivery()` before any send attempt. On success, `ackDelivery()` removes the entry (`deliver.ts:1367`). On throw, `failDelivery()` bumps `retryCount` and **leaves it pending** (`delivery-queue-storage.ts:137-144`).

  **What created the pending entries in #88955**: The `"Outbound not configured for channel: qqbot"` throw fired from `createChannelHandler` (`deliver.ts:223-231`) when `resolveOutboundChannelPluginForDelivery` returned a setup-only plugin with no send methods. This throw occurs **outside** the per-payload try/catch (`deliver.ts:1451`), so it propagated to the queue-cleanup catch → `failDelivery` → entry left pending. The resolver fix eliminates this throw by filtering setup-only entries via `channelPluginCanSend()`.

  **Why transient QQ API failures also do not create pending entries**: The qqbot engine **swallows** HTTP/network errors into returned `{error}` result objects instead of throwing (`outbound.ts:301-304, 313-318`). The delivery core text branch records "sent" purely on `deliveredResults.length > 0` with no message-id identity gate (`deliver.ts:1772-1778`), so a swallowed engine error yields a "sent" outcome → `ackDelivery` → entry removed, not pending.

  **The drain's real value**: It is a belt-and-suspenders safeguard for the **upgrade path** — entries left pending (with `lastError: "Outbound not configured for channel: qqbot"`) by an unfixed build are drained immediately after a fixed gateway reconnects. The `bypassBackoff` predicate explicitly matches this exact error string (`/outbound not configured/i`), ensuring these entries skip retry backoff and are retried on the first reconnect event. If the retry still fails (e.g., the plugin is genuinely misconfigured), `isPermanentDeliveryError` (`delivery-queue-recovery.ts:79`) moves the entry to `failed/` — no infinite retry loop. `MAX_RETRIES` is also enforced before the bypass branch (`delivery-queue-recovery.ts:544`). The drain also covers the narrow crash-in-enqueue-to-ack window handled by `recoverPendingDeliveries` at startup (`delivery-queue-recovery.ts:604`). The drain selection filter (`channel === "qqbot"` + account id match + bypassBackoff for `"Outbound not configured"`, `channel.test.ts:90-152`) and the reconnect-drain integration logic (`delivery-queue.reconnect-drain.test.ts`) are exercised by 35 unit tests (all passing).

  **Seeded pending-drain proof**: To validate the drain recovery path end-to-end, a pending delivery entry was manually seeded into the SQLite delivery queue to simulate the upgrade scenario (entries left by an unfixed build):

  1. Inserted a pending entry: `id=seeded-drain-test-001`, `channel=qqbot`, `accountId=default`, `lastError="Outbound not configured for channel: qqbot"`, `retry_count=1`
  2. Restarted gateway: `openclaw gateway restart`
  3. Observed recovery sequence in logs:

  ```
  00:30:00.353 [gateway] http server listening (8 plugins: ..., qqbot, ...; 1.9s)
  00:30:00.987 [delivery-recovery] Found 1 pending delivery entries — starting recovery
  00:30:01.567 [qqbot] [qqbot:api] <<< Status: 200 OK
  00:30:02.703 [delivery-recovery] Recovered delivery seeded-drain-test-001 on qqbot
  00:30:02.703 [delivery-recovery] Delivery recovery complete: 1 recovered, 0 failed, 0 skipped (max retries), 0 deferred (backoff)
  00:30:02.816 [qqbot] [default] WebSocket connected
  00:30:02.952 [qqbot] [qqbot:default] Gateway ready
  ```

  4. Verified entry removed from DB: `SELECT count(*) FROM delivery_queue_entries WHERE channel='qqbot' AND status='pending'` → **0**
  5. Zero `"Outbound not configured"` errors after restart

  **Key observation**: The `0 deferred (backoff)` in the recovery summary confirms the `bypassBackoff` predicate (`/outbound not configured/i`) correctly matched the seeded entry's `lastError`, allowing immediate retry without waiting for backoff. The entry was successfully delivered (HTTP 200) and acked (removed from queue).

  **Note**: The startup `recoverPendingDeliveries` path (`delivery-queue-recovery.ts:604`) executed before the qqbot `onReady` callback (`00:30:00.987` vs `00:30:02.952`), so startup recovery handled the entry before the reconnect drain fired. Both paths share the same underlying drain logic and bypass predicates; the startup path simply runs first. A naturally-occurring live drain on the `onReady` path cannot be reliably reproduced because the fix removes the deterministic failure that created those entries, and startup recovery pre-empts the reconnect drain for entries present at boot. Multi-shard configurations were not tested.

target.title: fix(safeguard): resolve compaction provider/model before registering runtime
target.body:
Fixes #57901.

## Summary

Two-part fix for the safeguard auto-compaction path ignoring `agents.defaults.compaction.model`:

1. **`extensions.ts`**: `buildEmbeddedExtensionFactories` now reuses the same compaction target resolver as `compaction-runtime-context.ts`, so the safeguard runtime gets the resolved compaction provider/model/auth instead of the active session model.
2. **`compaction-safeguard.ts`**: swaps precedence in the hook from `ctx.model ?? runtime?.model` to `runtime?.model ?? ctx.model`. Configured compaction model now wins when present; falls back to session model when no override is set.

## Why the previous PR (#57903) wasn't enough

Per the maintainer Codex review on this issue, the explicit `/compact` and context-engine compaction paths were already correct on main. The closed PR #57903 only swapped the precedence in the hook. The remaining bug — and what this PR fixes — is the embedded-extension runtime, which was still being populated with the active session model at registration time. So even with the precedence swap, the safeguard runtime had the wrong `runtime?.model` to compare against.

## Tests

**769 tests pass, 0 errors, 0 failed.**

New regression: `extensions.test.ts` and `compaction-safeguard.test.ts` cover the case where `ctx.model` = session model (e.g., Opus) and `agents.defaults.compaction.model` = different (e.g., Sonnet). Asserts the configured compaction model wins.

Maintainer review bar (per [comment 4336639752](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/57901#issuecomment-4336639752)) — all four guardrails covered:
- `/compact` not regressed
- Context-engine path not regressed
- Auth-profile dropping when providers change still works
- No-override fallback (when `agents.defaults.compaction.model` is unset) still falls back to session model

## Stats

- CHANGELOG.md untouched (release generation owns it per repo policy)

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Safeguard auto-compaction was ignoring `agents.defaults.compaction.model`: the embedded-extension runtime was registered with the active session model regardless of any configured compaction override (#57901). This PR makes `buildEmbeddedExtensionFactories` call `resolveSafeguardRuntimeTarget`, which routes through `resolveEmbeddedCompactionTarget` + `resolveModelWithRegistry`, and flips the precedence in the hook to `runtime?.model ?? ctx.model`. Inline-only models defined under `models.providers.*.models` are honored, and a misconfigured/missing override now triggers a `log.warn(...)` instead of silently registering `model: undefined`.

**Real environment tested:** exe.dev VM, `openclaw/openclaw` fork `joeykrug/openclaw`, worktree at `.../pr-73704-58823/pr-73704`, branch `fix/57901-safeguard-compaction-model` at HEAD `a7e454ae59b3850786ccf15cf9f9e2ec0d779948` (after merging `upstream/main` into the PR branch). Node `v24.15.0`, pnpm `11.1.0`. Sources read live from `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/extensions.ts`, `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/compaction-runtime-context.ts`, `src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard-runtime.ts`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

1. `pnpm install --prefer-offline --ignore-scripts` (worktree bootstrap, `SHARP_IGNORE_GLOBAL_LIBVIPS=1`).
2. Added a standalone real-behavior proof at `scripts/proof/pr-73704-compaction-model.mjs` that imports the actual patched source — `buildEmbeddedExtensionFactories`, `resolveSafeguardRuntimeTarget`, `resolveEmbeddedCompactionTarget`, and `getCompactionSafeguardRuntime` — and exercises three scenarios end-to-end (no vitest, no mocks of the function under test).
3. Ran: `node --import tsx scripts/proof/pr-73704-compaction-model.mjs` from the worktree root. Captured terminal output verbatim below.
4. Also ran the focused regression gates against the merged tree: `pnpm tsgo:core` (EXIT=0), `pnpm tsgo:core:test` (EXIT=0), `pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/extensions.test.ts src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent.auth-profile-propagation.test.ts` (Tests: 99 passed across 3 files), `pnpm lint:core` (0 warnings, 0 errors).

**Evidence after fix:** terminal stdout from the live `node --import tsx scripts/proof/pr-73704-compaction-model.mjs` run against the patched source (exit code 0):

```text
$ node --import tsx scripts/proof/pr-73704-compaction-model.mjs
--- Scenario A: configured compaction model overrides session model ---
  session provider/model in : anthropic/claude-opus-4-7
  configured compaction      : anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6 (from agents.defaults.compaction.model)
  resolveEmbeddedCompactionTarget => provider="anthropic" model="claude-sonnet-4-6"
  OK   pure target provider = "anthropic"
  OK   pure target model = "claude-sonnet-4-6"
    modelRegistry.find("anthropic", "claude-sonnet-4-6")
  resolveSafeguardRuntimeTarget => provider="anthropic" modelId="claude-sonnet-4-6" model.id="claude-sonnet-4-6"
  OK   runtime target provider = "anthropic"
  OK   runtime target modelId = "claude-sonnet-4-6"
  OK   runtime target model.id = "claude-sonnet-4-6"
  OK   runtime target model.provider = "anthropic"
  OK   runtime target model.id is NOT session id = true
    modelRegistry.find("anthropic", "claude-sonnet-4-6")
  registered safeguard runtime  : provider="anthropic" model.id="claude-sonnet-4-6" contextWindowTokens=200000
  OK   registered runtime model.id = "claude-sonnet-4-6"
  OK   registered runtime model.provider = "anthropic"
  OK   registered runtime model.id != session model id = true

--- Scenario B: no compaction.model configured -> session model preserved ---
  registered safeguard runtime  : model.id="claude-opus-4-7" (session id was "claude-opus-4-7")
  OK   session model.id preserved when no override = "claude-opus-4-7"
  OK   registered runtime model is session model (no override) = true

--- Scenario C: configured compaction model missing from registry ---
[agent/embedded] Configured safeguard compaction model "anthropic/claude-typo-4-6" could not be resolved; falling back to the session model.
  unresolved target             : provider="anthropic" modelId="claude-typo-4-6" model=undefined (safeguard surfaces miss)
  OK   provider stays as configured = "anthropic"
  OK   modelId stays as configured = "claude-typo-4-6"
  OK   model resolves to undefined on miss = undefined

PROOF PASSED: configured compaction model wins over session model in safeguard registration.
```

Supplementary CI gates against the merged tree (all green):

```text
$ pnpm tsgo:core
EXIT=0  (no diagnostics)

$ pnpm tsgo:core:test
EXIT=0  (no diagnostics)

$ pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/extensions.test.ts src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent.auth-profile-propagation.test.ts
 Test Files  3 passed (3)
      Tests  99 passed (99)

$ pnpm lint:core
Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.
```

**Observed result after fix:** the live `node --import tsx` proof script terminates with `PROOF PASSED` and exit code 0. Scenario A: with `agents.defaults.compaction.model = "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6"` and session model `anthropic/claude-opus-4-7`, the safeguard runtime registered on the SessionManager carries `model.id = "claude-sonnet-4-6"` and `model.provider = "anthropic"` — the configured compaction model, not the session model. Scenario B: with no override, the safeguard runtime keeps the session model (`claude-opus-4-7`). Scenario C: with a missing/typo override (`anthropic/claude-typo-4-6`), `resolveSafeguardRuntimeTarget` returns `model: undefined`, the runtime carries the configured provider/modelId, and `[agent/embedded] Configured safeguard compaction model ... could not be resolved; falling back to the session model.` is emitted on stderr/stdout. Behavior matches the precedence inverted in `compaction-safeguard.ts` (`const model = runtime?.model ?? ctx.model;`).

**What was not tested:** no live provider API call was made — proof stops at safeguard runtime registration + target resolution against the actual patched source. The end-to-end "real compaction summary served by the configured model over the wire" requires a long-running session with an active provider account; that was not run in this VM. The script also does not exercise the `extensions.test.ts` greptile P1 path through vitest (covered separately by the existing test file and the supplementary `pnpm test` run above).

target.title: fix(issue-template): split logs and screenshots into separate fields
target.body:
## What

Splits the **Logs, screenshots, and evidence** field in the bug report issue template into two separate fields:

- **Logs** - retains `render: shell` for syntax highlighting
- **Screenshots, recordings, and evidence** - a plain textarea that accepts image attachments

## Why

The original field described itself as accepting logs, screenshots, and recordings, but `render: shell` forces the textarea into code-block mode which silently prevents image attachments. Users dragging or pasting a screenshot into that field would find it never attaches.

The field was trying to do two incompatible things at once. Splitting them fixes the screenshot UX without losing shell syntax highlighting for logs.

## Change

```diff
  - type: textarea
    id: logs
    attributes:
-     label: Logs, screenshots, and evidence
-     description: Include the redacted logs, screenshots, recordings, docs, or version comparisons that support the grounded answers above.
+     label: Logs
+     description: Include the redacted logs or version comparisons that support the grounded answers above.
      render: shell
+ - type: textarea
+   id: screenshots_evidence
+   attributes:
+     label: Screenshots, recordings, and evidence
+     description: Include screenshots, recordings, docs, or other evidence that support the grounded answers above.
```
## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Screenshots and images pasted into the "Logs, screenshots, and evidence" field silently fail to attach because `render: shell` disables file attachment support in GitHub issue forms.

- **Real environment tested**: https://github.com/d1rshan/openclaw — fork with fix applied to default branch, tested via GitHub's live issue form UI.

- **Exact steps or command run after fix**:
  1. Opened a new issue on the fork at https://github.com/d1rshan/openclaw/issues/new/choose
  2. Selected the bug report template
  3. Dragged a screen recording into the new "Screenshots, recordings, and evidence" field
  4. Confirmed the recording attached successfully (markdown attachment link appeared in the field)

- **Evidence after fix**: 

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/12a0965c-0fac-43ea-aded-ab4c6cd1368e

- **Observed result after fix**: Recording attached successfully to the "Screenshots, recordings, and evidence" field. The "Logs" field retains shell syntax highlighting as expected.

- **What was not tested**: None

- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression
