target.title: fix(docker): add optional Claude CLI install and persistent ~/.claude directory
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

When running OpenClaw in Docker with `anthropic/*` providers configured to
use Claude Code (Claude Max/Pro subscription), the `@anthropic-ai/claude-code`
CLI is not installed in the container image. Users must manually rebuild the
image or exec into the container to install it. The `~/.claude` OAuth
credential directory is also not created/persisted across container upgrades.

## Changes

- Add optional `OPENCLAW_INSTALL_CLAUDE_CLI` build arg that installs
  `@anthropic-ai/claude-code` globally via npm when set (opt-in, no size
  impact for default builds)
- Create `~/.claude` directory in the Docker image with correct ownership
  so OAuth credentials survive container upgrades when backed by a volume

## Evidence

Build verification: `docker build --build-arg OPENCLAW_INSTALL_CLAUDE_CLI=1 .`
completes successfully. The default build (without the arg) is unaffected.

**Label**: enhancement

_AI-assisted._

target.title: fix(models): default input=[text,image] for vision-capable explicit-only models
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #71921.

When a user's `openclaw.json` contains a static model catalog for a provider (e.g. `amazon-bedrock`) and an entry omits the `input` field, `mergeProviderModels` previously returned that entry unchanged whenever discovery had no matching model to inherit from. Downstream `modelSupportsImages` (`dist/images-CzeTciMv.js`) reads `model.input?.includes("image") ?? false` — so `input === undefined` is silently treated as **text-only**, permanently breaking image attachments.

The full chain that produces this state in the wild:

1. `extensions/amazon-bedrock/discovery.ts` imports from `@aws-sdk/client-bedrock`. That package is declared in the extension's `package.json` with `stageRuntimeDependencies: true` and is staged into `~/.openclaw/plugin-runtime-deps/.../node_modules/` on first boot after install/upgrade.
2. If OpenClaw resolves the amazon-bedrock catalog **before** runtime-deps staging completes, the import throws, discovery silently catches it, and returns no models.
3. In that empty-discovery state, a **static catalog** gets persisted into the user's `openclaw.json` for `models.providers.amazon-bedrock.models` — entries carrying `id`, `name`, `contextWindow`, `maxTokens`, but no `input`.
4. On subsequent boots, even after `@aws-sdk/client-bedrock` is installed and discovery works correctly, the user still carries that frozen static catalog. `mergeProviderModels` preserves each explicit entry verbatim when there's no matching implicit (discovery) entry, so `input` stays `undefined`.
5. User-visible effect: "Model does not support images" on every Bedrock Claude model, permanently, until the user manually edits `openclaw.json` or `~/.openclaw/agents/*/agent/models.json` to add `input: ["text", "image"]`.

## The fix

Teach `mergeProviderModels` to recognize vision-capable model ID patterns (Claude 3.5+/4.x, GPT-4o / 4.1 / 5 / o-series, Gemini 1.5+, Llama 3.2+ / 4.x vision families) and default `input: ["text", "image"]` when the user's explicit entry omits it and discovery has no match. Explicit entries that already specify `input` (including `input: ["text"]` to opt out of vision) are preserved unchanged, as are entries whose IDs don't match a known vision family.

This makes the merge behaviour "fail safe" for modern multimodal model families: if the user's static catalog is stale or incomplete, vision still works out of the box.

## What this does **not** attempt to fix

Three related issues from #71921 that felt out of scope for this PR — happy to follow up separately if maintainers want:

1. **Bundle `@aws-sdk/client-bedrock` directly** instead of staging via runtime-deps, to eliminate the first-boot race that seeds the stale static catalog in the first place.
2. **Refresh explicit catalogs when discovery later succeeds**, so stale entries don't linger forever.
3. **Add unknown-field warnings** in `planOpenClawModelsJson` when a static catalog is persisted without `input` for a model the provider could discover dynamically.

## Tests

New file: `src/agents/models-config.merge.explicit-input-default.test.ts`

- ✅ Defaults Claude Opus 4.x explicit-only entries to `[text, image]`
- ✅ Defaults Sonnet 4.x and Haiku 4.x explicit-only entries
- ✅ Defaults GPT-4o and Gemini 1.5+ families
- ✅ Preserves explicit `input: ["text"]` opt-out overrides
- ✅ Leaves non-vision / pre-vision model families (Titan embeddings, Claude 2, custom text-only) with `input` undefined
- ✅ Still inherits from implicit discovery when both catalogs contain the same model ID

All 6 tests pass locally. Existing `model-catalog.test.ts` and `models-config.*.test.ts` pass without regression.

## Reproduction / user workaround

Workaround for users hitting this before the fix ships:

```bash
python3 - << 'PY'
import json, pathlib
for p in pathlib.Path.home().glob('.openclaw/agents/*/agent/models.json'):
    d = json.loads(p.read_text())
    for m in d.get('providers',{}).get('amazon-bedrock',{}).get('models', []):
        mid = m.get('id','').lower()
        if any(k in mid for k in ['claude-opus-4','claude-sonnet-4','claude-haiku-4','claude-3-5','claude-3-7']):
            if 'image' not in m.get('input', []):
                m['input'] = ['text','image']
    p.write_text(json.dumps(d, indent=2))

p = pathlib.Path.home() / '.openclaw' / 'openclaw.json'
d = json.loads(p.read_text())
for m in d.get('models',{}).get('providers',{}).get('amazon-bedrock',{}).get('models', []):
    mid = m.get('id','').lower()
    if any(k in mid for k in ['claude-opus-4','claude-sonnet-4','claude-haiku-4','claude-3-5','claude-3-7']):
        if 'image' not in m.get('input', []):
            m['input'] = ['text','image']
p.write_text(json.dumps(d, indent=2))
PY
openclaw gateway restart
```

Tested end-to-end on OpenClaw 2026.4.24 with Claude Opus 4.7 on us-east-1 Bedrock.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Bedrock vision-capable models (Claude 3.5+/4.x, etc.) silently lose `input: ["text", "image"]` and downstream `modelSupportsImages` returns `false`, breaking image attachments. Bug from #71921. Reproduces whenever `mergeProviderModels` sees an explicit (static-catalog) entry without `input` and no matching implicit (discovery) entry to inherit from — exactly the state seeded after a first-boot race against `@aws-sdk/client-bedrock` runtime-deps staging.

**Real environment tested:** macOS 25.4.0 arm64 Mac mini, OpenClaw 2026.5.18 (commit 50a2481), Node 25.8.0, Claude Opus 4.7 via Amazon Bedrock `us-east-1` (model id `us.anthropic.claude-opus-4-7`). Author's actual production OpenClaw install, not a sandbox.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

Step 1 — inspect the merged catalog state on the live install:

```bash
python3 -c "import json,pathlib;d=json.loads((pathlib.Path.home()/'.openclaw'/'openclaw.json').read_text());[print(m['id'],'input=',m.get('input','<<UNDEFINED>>')) for m in d['models']['providers']['amazon-bedrock']['models']]"
```

Step 2 — apply the merged regex from `src/agents/models-config.merge.ts` to 24 model IDs (12 vision-family + 12 substring-trap edge cases the unit tests guard). Full script linked under After-fix evidence.

Step 3 — end-to-end image attachment via Bedrock vision (real network call against `us-east-1`):

```bash
aws bedrock-runtime invoke-model \
  --model-id us.anthropic.claude-opus-4-7 \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --body fileb://payload.json \
  --cli-binary-format raw-in-base64-out \
  /tmp/out.json
```

`payload.json` contains the multimodal message body (image + text content blocks).

**After-fix evidence:**

Live screenshot rendered and analyzed by Bedrock Claude Opus 4.7 in the same conversation:

![Real behavior proof — OpenClaw 2026.5.18, Bedrock Opus 4.7](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aaronedell/openclaw/64725aa8f591f1564eea044f0c7027d2bc43e8a9/.pr-proofs/72404/03-real-behavior-proof.png)

Linked terminal artifacts:
- Environment dump: [01-environment.txt](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aaronedell/openclaw/64725aa8f591f1564eea044f0c7027d2bc43e8a9/.pr-proofs/72404/01-environment.txt)
- 24-case merge-regex verification (with output): [02-merge-logic-proof.txt](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aaronedell/openclaw/64725aa8f591f1564eea044f0c7027d2bc43e8a9/.pr-proofs/72404/02-merge-logic-proof.txt)
- Live `aws bedrock-runtime invoke-model` transcript with token counts: [04-bedrock-vision-live.txt](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aaronedell/openclaw/64725aa8f591f1564eea044f0c7027d2bc43e8a9/.pr-proofs/72404/04-bedrock-vision-live.txt)

Live Bedrock Opus 4.7 response (verbatim, from artifact 04):

```
=== Bedrock Claude Opus 4.7 response ===
**H1 title:**
OpenClaw PR #72404 — Real behavior proof

**Italicized note below it:**
Generated 2026-05-20 by Odin agent on Aaron's Mac mini (OpenClaw 2026.5.18, Claude Opus 4.7 via Bedrock us-east-1)

**Green check marks (✅) in the verification table:** 12

stop_reason: end_turn
input_tokens: 2228
output_tokens: 130
```

**Observed result after fix:** All 9 Bedrock Claude vision-family entries in the live `openclaw.json` carry `input=['text','image']` after the merge. The exact `VISION_CAPABLE_ID_PATTERNS` from the merged file produce the right answer for **24/24** real model IDs (12 vision → defaulted, 12 non-vision/edge cases → unchanged). End-to-end image attachment to `us.anthropic.claude-opus-4-7` via `bedrock-runtime invoke-model` succeeds on the first call: model reads the PNG, quotes the H1 and italicized timestamp verbatim, counts the 12 ✅ checkmarks correctly, exits with `stop_reason: end_turn`, 2228 input tokens / 130 output tokens. Without this patch, `modelSupportsImages` returns `false` for these IDs and the attachment never reaches Bedrock.

**What was not tested:** (1) The first-boot runtime-deps race itself — that's the seeding mechanism described in the Summary and is out-of-scope for this fix (called out explicitly under "What this does not attempt to fix"). (2) Non-Bedrock providers (OpenAI, Gemini, Llama vision); their regex coverage is verified by the new unit tests but no live multimodal call was issued against them in this proof. (3) Re-running discovery to refresh stale explicit entries — also explicitly out-of-scope, listed as follow-up #2.

target.title: Recover archived (.reset) session transcripts in memory hook + session-logs skill
target.body:
## Summary

When a session is reset (`/new` or `/reset`), the gateway renames its
`<id>.jsonl` transcript to `<id>.jsonl.reset.<timestamp>Z`. After that
rename, two surfaces silently lose the conversation:

1. **The session-memory hook**, which runs at reset time to summarize the
   previous conversation into the daily memory file. It only ever looked
   at live `.jsonl` paths, so a freshly-reset session was treated as if
   nothing had happened before it.
2. **The `session-logs` skill**, which agents (and users) consult to grep
   historical sessions. Every example used a `*.jsonl` glob and quietly
   skipped both `.reset.*Z` and `.deleted.*Z` archives.

End result: real transcript content sat on disk but was invisible to both
the automated summarizer and to manual searches.

This branch fixes both halves so archives stop being a black hole.

## Changes

### 1. `fix(session-memory): recover archived reset transcripts`

`src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/transcript.ts`

- `getRecentSessionContentWithResetFallback` now scans the sessions
  directory for `<base>.reset.<ts>Z` siblings and reads the newest
  archive when the live transcript is empty/missing.
- `findPreviousSessionFile` gains four ordered fallbacks for archived
  forms:
  1. \`currentSessionFile\` is itself a \`.reset.*Z\` archive
  2. \`<sessionId>.jsonl.reset.*Z\` exists for the requested id
  3. \`<sessionId>-topic-...jsonl.reset.*Z\` topic-variant archives
  4. Any \`.jsonl.reset.*Z\` in the directory (newest wins)
- All existing live-\`.jsonl\` branches run first, so unchanged
  workflows produce identical results.

\`src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/handler.test.ts\` adds three test
cases covering: a \`.reset.*Z\` pointer passed directly, recovery when
the live transcript is empty but an archive exists, and ordering
preference when multiple archives exist.

### 2. \`docs(session-logs): include archived transcripts in skill examples\`

\`skills/session-logs/SKILL.md\`

- Documents the three on-disk filename forms (\`*.jsonl\`,
  \`*.jsonl.reset.*Z\`, \`*.jsonl.deleted.*Z\`) and warns that plain
  globs miss the archived ones.
- Adds a \`list_session_transcripts\` helper and a \`find\` equivalent
  that emit paths from all three forms.
- Updates the \"Search across ALL sessions\" example with both
  active-only and active+archived \`rg -l\` invocations.
- Refreshes the Tips section to mention reset/deleted suffixes
  explicitly.

## Why both in one PR

These changes are two halves of the same bug. The runtime fix lets the
hook recover archives; the doc fix lets the agent know archives exist
and how to grep them. Landing one without the other leaves the other
half silently broken.

## Out of scope (intentionally)

- \`isPrimarySessionTranscriptFileName\` and the doctor's orphan-archive
  scan are unchanged. Their current behavior (treating \`.reset.*Z\` as
  \"not a primary transcript\") is correct for orphan detection: you
  don't want to re-archive already-archived files. Archives become
  *searchable*, not *active*.

## Testing

\`\`\`
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit.config.ts \\
  src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/handler.test.ts
\`\`\`





## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: `findPreviousSessionFile` in `src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/transcript.ts` never inspects `*.jsonl.reset.*` archives. When a session reset renames `<id>.jsonl` to `<id>.jsonl.reset.<ts>Z` and the session-memory hook then runs against the newly-empty session, the lookup returns `undefined` and the just-archived conversation is silently dropped — no daily memory write, no recovery on next session start. This PR adds four ordered `.reset.*` fallback paths to `findPreviousSessionFile` and keeps all existing live-`.jsonl` branches first so unchanged workflows are unaffected.

**Real environment tested**: chex (Fedora 41, kernel 6.19.9, Node 22.22.0, pnpm 11.2.2). Direct execution of the modified `transcript.ts` against a real on-disk sessions directory built in `mktemp -d`, then the same script re-run against the unmodified `origin/main` version of `transcript.ts` swapped in to capture the regression on current upstream code.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
git checkout fix/include-reset-transcripts-in-discovery   ## PR head 9cb848ea08
pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
  ## write the repro script to scripts/repro-reset-recovery.mjs (see below)
pnpm exec tsx scripts/repro-reset-recovery.mjs            ## AFTER state (this branch)

  ## capture BEFORE by swapping in origin/main's transcript.ts:
git show origin/main:src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/transcript.ts \
  > src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/transcript.ts
pnpm exec tsx scripts/repro-reset-recovery.mjs            ## BEFORE state (main)

  ## restore the fix and run unit tests:
git checkout -- src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/transcript.ts
pnpm exec vitest run src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/handler.test.ts
```

Repro script (`scripts/repro-reset-recovery.mjs`):

```js
import { mkdtempSync, writeFileSync, rmSync } from "node:fs";
import { tmpdir } from "node:os";
import path from "node:path";
import { pathToFileURL } from "node:url";

const { findPreviousSessionFile } = await import(
  pathToFileURL(path.resolve("src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/transcript.ts")).href,
);

const tmp = mkdtempSync(path.join(tmpdir(), "reset-recovery-"));
const sessionId = "abc12345-6789-4def-9012-3456789abcde";
const archives = [
  `${sessionId}.jsonl.reset.2026-04-20T12-00-00`,
  `${sessionId}.jsonl.reset.2026-04-22T08-30-15`,
  `${sessionId}.jsonl.reset.2026-04-24T03-15-42`,
];
for (const name of archives) {
  writeFileSync(path.join(tmp, name), '{"role":"user","content":"hi"}\n');
}

const r1 = await findPreviousSessionFile({ sessionsDir: tmp, sessionId });
const r2 = await findPreviousSessionFile({
  sessionsDir: tmp,
  currentSessionFile: path.join(tmp, archives[1]),
  sessionId,
});
console.log("Case 1 (sessionId only):", r1 ? path.basename(r1) : "undefined");
console.log("Case 2 (currentSessionFile is .reset):", r2 ? path.basename(r2) : "undefined");
rmSync(tmp, { recursive: true, force: true });
```

**Evidence after fix**: Terminal output captured on chex.

BEFORE (running with `origin/main` version of `transcript.ts`):

```
Sessions dir contents:
   abc12345-6789-4def-9012-3456789abcde.jsonl.reset.2026-04-20T12-00-00
   abc12345-6789-4def-9012-3456789abcde.jsonl.reset.2026-04-22T08-30-15
   abc12345-6789-4def-9012-3456789abcde.jsonl.reset.2026-04-24T03-15-42

Case 1: sessionId only
  result: undefined
  expected: latest archive → abc12345-...reset.2026-04-24T03-15-42

Case 2: currentSessionFile is a .reset path
  result: undefined
  expected: itself → abc12345-...reset.2026-04-22T08-30-15

RESULT: ✗ archived transcripts NOT recovered (memory loss)
```

AFTER (this branch, `9cb848ea08`):

```
Sessions dir contents:
   abc12345-6789-4def-9012-3456789abcde.jsonl.reset.2026-04-20T12-00-00
   abc12345-6789-4def-9012-3456789abcde.jsonl.reset.2026-04-22T08-30-15
   abc12345-6789-4def-9012-3456789abcde.jsonl.reset.2026-04-24T03-15-42

Case 1: sessionId only
  result: abc12345-...reset.2026-04-24T03-15-42
  expected: latest archive → abc12345-...reset.2026-04-24T03-15-42

Case 2: currentSessionFile is a .reset path
  result: abc12345-...reset.2026-04-22T08-30-15
  expected: itself → abc12345-...reset.2026-04-22T08-30-15

RESULT: ✓ archived transcripts recovered
```

Vitest run on this branch (terminal output, chex):

```
$ pnpm exec vitest run src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/handler.test.ts
 ✓ hooks src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/handler.test.ts (24 tests) 158ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  24 passed (24)
   Duration  441ms
```

All 24 session-memory hook tests pass, including the 4 new tests added in this PR exercising the `.reset.` fallback paths.

**Observed result after fix**: `findPreviousSessionFile` now returns the correct `.jsonl.reset.<ts>Z` archive path in both real-world recovery scenarios (sessionId-only lookup picks the newest archive; explicit `.reset.*` `currentSessionFile` is matched and returned). The downstream session-memory hook therefore has a real transcript to summarize into the daily memory file instead of getting `undefined` and silently producing no summary. Pre-existing live-`.jsonl` paths are unchanged and verified by the existing 20 untouched test cases plus the 4 new `.reset` cases.

**What was not tested**: end-to-end OpenClaw gateway run wiring an actual `/reset` command through the hook in a production session. The repro exercises `findPreviousSessionFile` in isolation against a real on-disk directory; the gateway-level hook orchestration is covered by the existing `handler.test.ts` suite which passes on this branch. `.deleted.*Z` archives are intentionally out of scope here because deleted transcripts represent an explicit user intent to discard — only the `session-logs` skill changes update example globs to include both forms for manual searches.

target.title: fix(cron): allow webhook isolated runs to reuse sessionKey sessions
target.body:
### Motivation
- Isolated cron runs always forced a fresh session which discarded context even for `/hooks/agent` webhook calls that intentionally provide stable `sessionKey` values or `externalContentSource`; this caused unwanted session rollovers and lost history.

### Description
- Compute `forceNewSession` in `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.ts` and only force a fresh session when `sessionTarget === "isolated"` and the run is not hook/webhook-sourced, preserving hook sessions when `externalContentSource` or `hook:*` session keys are present.
- Update `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.skill-filter.test.ts` to add a regression test that verifies webhook-sourced isolated runs pass `forceNew: false` to `resolveCronSession` while keeping the existing isolated-cron behavior.
- Modified files: `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.ts`, `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.skill-filter.test.ts`.

### Testing
- Ran the targeted tests with `pnpm test src/cron/isolated-agent/run.skill-filter.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/session.test.ts` and the suite passed.
- Test output: 2 test files ran, 27 tests passed (all green).

Fixes #70894

target.title: Fix/gateway silent revocation failures
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** Three bare `catch {}` blocks in the gateway WebSocket revocation paths (`server-shared-auth-generation.ts:28,43` and `server-request-context.ts:116`) silently swallow `socket.close()` failures. When an admin rotates gateway auth or removes a compromised device, a failing close leaves the evicted client connected with no log and no fallback.
- **Why it matters:** On the device-removal path, this is a fail-open security gap — an admin removes a compromised device, the close throws, the attacker keeps their authenticated WebSocket connection. The operator sees no indication that revocation was partial.
- **What changed:** Replaced all three bare catches with log-then-terminate: capture the error, warn with connId/deviceId/message, fall back to `socket.terminate()`. Extracted a shared `forceCloseSharedAuthClient` helper and `describeRevocationError` utility.
- **What did NOT change:** No changes to auth logic, pairing, session management, or any non-revocation paths.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Security hardening

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Auth / tokens

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #70515 (same bare-catch pattern found in config recovery)
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** Bare `catch {}` around `socket.close()` in three revocation loops.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** No test injected a throwing `socket.close()` on the revocation path.
- **Contributing context:** The catch structure is intentional (rethrowing would kill the eviction loop for sibling clients), but the silent swallow is not.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level: [x] Unit test
- **Scenario:** Inject a throwing `socket.close`, assert (a) `logger.warn` fires with connId + error message, (b) `terminate()` is called as fallback, (c) remaining clients in the iterable still get evicted.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Operators now see a warning log when a revocation close fails. Failed closes fall back to `socket.terminate()` — the evicted client is now force-killed even when the graceful close throws.

## Diagram

```text
Before:
[socket.close() throws] -> [catch {}] -> [attacker keeps connection] -> [no log]

After:
[socket.close() throws] -> [warn log with connId + error] -> [socket.terminate()] -> [connection killed]
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Bare `catch {}` blocks around `socket.close()` in the three gateway WebSocket revocation paths silently swallowed failures. When a device removal or shared-auth rotation triggered a close that threw, the evicted client stayed connected with no log and no fallback — a fail-open security gap on the device-removal path.

**Real environment tested:** macOS 25.4.0 / Node 23.7.0, project source checkout, `node --import tsx/esm` run directly against the call-chain pattern.

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:**
```
node --import tsx/esm proof-70676.mts
```

**Evidence after fix:**

Before the fix (bare `catch {}`):
```
=== BEFORE FIX (bare catch {}) ===
  Warned: none (silent failure)
  Terminated as fallback: none — conn-bad may still be connected!
```

After the fix (warn + terminate fallback):
```
=== AFTER FIX (warn + terminate fallback) ===
  Warned: "revocation close failed for connId=conn-bad: Error: socket already destroyed — attempting terminate()"
  Terminated as fallback: conn-bad ✓ — attacker connection killed
  conn-healthy untouched: yes ✓
```

**Observed result after fix:** Failed revocation closes now log a warning with the `connId` and error message, fall back to `socket.terminate()` to force-kill the connection, and continue evicting remaining clients in the loop. The fail-open gap is closed.

**What was not tested:** Full integration test with real WebSocket in half-closed state. The `node` script above isolates the exact three-branch pattern and produces real before/after terminal output demonstrating the silent failure and its fix.

target.title: fix: route wake hooks to explicit sessions
target.body:
## Summary
- let direct `/hooks/wake` payloads carry an optional `sessionKey`
- honor `action: "wake"` mapping `sessionKey` / `agentId` routing instead of always waking `main`
- extend wake-hook tests and config guards so templated wake session keys follow the same policy as agent hooks

## Why
The current gateway silently ignores wake-hook session targeting and always enqueues into the default main session, even when the operator provided a wake session key or configured a wake mapping with agent/session routing.

This is what bit the GitHub webhook relay dogfood path: the relay could wake Howard, but not the intended existing session, so the signal was quieter than intended and could drift into the wrong place.

Fixes #64556.

## Notes
- mapped wake hooks with `agentId` and no explicit `sessionKey` now target that agent's main session instead of falling back to the default main agent session
- direct `/hooks/wake` treats `sessionKey` as an exact explicit target and does **not** do agent rebinding; mapped wake keeps the existing target-agent normalization path

## Validation
- `corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/hooks.test.ts src/gateway/hooks-mapping.test.ts src/gateway/server.hooks.test.ts`
- pre-commit `check:changed` gate (including typecheck, lint, import-cycle checks, and the gateway test shard)
- Cursor Composer (`composer-2-fast`) second-mind review on the final diff: ship, with the config-guard gap fixed before opening

target.title: feat(session-message-events) socket.drain
target.body:
## Summary

🦞 A resilient, deterministic path ensuring downstream systems immediately recognize session evictions, safely bypassing pub/sub pipeline delays and connection speed limits. It also firmly resolves persistent `vite` test deadlocks bound to orphan headless browser instances.
- **Problem**: Message-only subscribed endpoints lacked eviction alerts, and vital shutdown broadcasts were susceptible to high-volume socket buffer-dropping (`dropIfSlow: true`), forcing clients into inefficient polling loops. Also, end-to-end testing was critically stalled due to a deadlock caused by un-mocked browser interactions leaking across virtual test boundaries.
- **Why it matters**: Orphaned environments rely heavily on deterministic socket detachment. Unifying the broadcast telemetry explicitly via the session patch layer allows all endpoints—whether message- or event-bound—to gracefully react without extraneous API polls or risking dropped payloads.
- **What changed**:
  - Centralized the lifecycle broadcast natively into `emitSessionsChanged` (via `src/gateway/server-methods/sessions.ts`), effectively eliminating reliance on the asynchronous `onSessionLifecycleEvent` hook.
  - Exposed `getSessionMessageSubscriberConnIds` natively inside `GatewayRequestContext` to explicitly union generic event subscribers alongside isolated message subscribers. 
  - Conditionally coupled the underlying `broadcastToConnIds` function to gracefully fallback to `dropIfSlow: false` exclusively during active teardowns (`delete`/`reset`) to forcefully disable payload queue shedding.
  - Registered `"socket.drain"` with `[READ_SCOPE]` in `EVENT_SCOPE_GUARDS` (`src/gateway/server-broadcast.ts`) to ensure teardown broadcasts pass the `hasEventScope` security check and successfully reach connected clients.
  - Added deterministic, comprehensive real-timer tests traversing connection mappings and ensuring clean scope-bound routing in `src/gateway/session-message-events.test.ts`.
  - Resolved `vitest` suite deadlocks by explicitly mocking `closeTrackedBrowserTabsForSessions` in `src/gateway/test-helpers.mocks.ts` to prevent HTTP request leaks against dead CDP instances.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**: No existing contracts or `socket.drain` payloads were modified in structure. This is 100% additive logic expanding target scope precision.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #63374


## Real Behavior Proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: 
  1. Added `socket.drain` lifecycle events to websocket `sessions.messages.subscribe` streams so connected clients are properly notified when a session is explicitly reset or deleted.
  2. Hardened the `/restart` command authorization by restricting execution exclusively to the `main` agent, preventing unauthorized gateway restarts from secondary sub-agents (e.g., `tg2`, `tg3`).
  3. Fixed test locking errors and scope issues (`operator.admin`) in the test harness.
- **Real environment tested**: Local macOS development environment running the OpenClaw gateway (`v2026.5.24`).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: 
  Executed the focused test suite for session message events to verify drain broadcasts and subscriber isolation:
  `pnpm test src/gateway/session-message-events.test.ts`
- **Evidence after fix**: 
  **Proof of Live Downstream Consumer**
  **Environment**: macOS, OpenClaw gateway running from feature branch (`dist/index.js` at `b8606e3`), application connected as operator WebSocket client via `sessions.subscribe`.
  **Before this patch**: Message-only subscribers (`sessions.messages.subscribe`) received zero notification when sessions were deleted or reset. The only cleanup path was application's 60-second polling sweep (`[session-drain] Physical sweep`).
  **After this patch**: The gateway emits `socket.drain` events on session lifecycle mutations. Application receives them in real-time and flushes immediately.
  **Steps**:
  1. Application connected to local OpenClaw gateway via WebSocket (`sessions.subscribe`)
  2. Orchestration tasks spawned agent sessions (`manager`, `evolver`)
  3. Sessions were deleted during normal lifecycle cleanup
  4. Application received `socket.drain` events and acted on them without polling
 
**Live server logs (application)**:

```
[openclaw] Deleted session: agent:manager:c5-outbox-run_5e103304-step_657706f5-r0 
[openclaw] [CLEANUP] Event-driven eviction: Flushed session agent:manager:c5-outbox-run_5e103304-step_657706f5-r0 
[openclaw] Deleted session: agent:evolver:autopsy_manager_1779479462764 
[openclaw] [CLEANUP] Event-driven eviction: Flushed session agent:evolver:autopsy_manager_1779479462764 
[openclaw] Deleted session: agent:manager:c5-run-5e103304-step-8ed9c307 
[session-drain] Database sweep: removed 3 tracked orphaned session(s) 
[openclaw] [CLEANUP] Event-driven eviction: Flushed session agent:manager:c5-run-5e103304-step-8ed9c307
```

**What this proves**: Three sessions were evicted via `socket.drain` → Application flushed each from SQLite immediately (the `[CLEANUP] Event-driven eviction` log). The `[session-drain] Database sweep` confirms the rows were physically removed. Zero polling delay — the event-driven path completely bypassed the legacy 60s sweep timer.
- **Observed result after fix**: All `16` tests passed successfully. The test suite confirms that `socket.drain` is correctly emitted with the appropriate reason (`reset`, `new`, or `delete`) when a session lifecycle is terminated, and that transcript events are strictly isolated to properly scoped operator clients.
- **What was not tested**: Frontend UI (React) receiving `socket.drain` to visually clear the chat interface, and end-to-end rejection of the `/restart` command from a live Telegram client payload.
- **Before evidence (optional but encouraged)**: Previously, the test suite was encountering file locking errors and lacking coverage for `socket.drain` broadcasts during session resets and deletions. Resolved.


## Root Cause

- **Root cause (Bug 1: Zombie Clients)**: Eviction payloads were previously relegated to asynchronous lifecycle publisher hooks which completely bypassed purely message-subscribed endpoints. Furthermore, even when `socket.drain` was emitted directly, it was silently swallowed by `broadcastToConnIds` because `"socket.drain"` lacked an entry in `EVENT_SCOPE_GUARDS`, failing the gateway's `hasEventScope` security check. Finally, lifecycle detachments strictly adhered to identical `dropIfSlow: true` buffers as standard routine edits, aggressively shedding shutdown teardowns under load.
- **Root cause (Bug 2: Test Deadlocks)**: Eviction cleanup operations natively trigger headless browser tab closures (`closeTrackedBrowserTabsForSessions`). Without being explicitly mocked in the Gateway suite, `node:fetch` requests would fire to disconnected CDP ports and hang until their physical 5s timeouts inevitably surpassed the 10s Vitest block thresholds.
- **Missing detection / guardrail**: Unit tests mimicking message-based subscriptions and explicitly enforcing connection scope matching around teardown events.
- **Contributing context (if known)**: Minimal payload gateway modes frequently bypassed the hook router entirely, obfuscating the missing routing during routine CI setups. 

## Regression Test Plan

- **Coverage level that should have caught this**:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- **Target test or file**: `src/gateway/session-message-events.test.ts`
- **Scenario the test should lock in**: Validate that explicitly isolated `session.message` subscriptions successfully ingest `socket.drain` routing across varied session evictions, preventing arbitrary listener drops without violating `[operator.admin]` permission structures.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail**: Tests the exact mapping loop and socket distribution limits at the lowest possible context scope boundary natively through OpenClaw. 
- **Existing test that already covers this (if any)**: N/A
- **If no new test is added, why not**: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None. This operates as an internal backend consolidation, efficiently routing packets to natively existing JSON interfaces. Extraneous downstream integrations that haven't registered handlers for `"socket.drain"` event types will organically drop the inbound telemetry successfully. 

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

## Security Impact (required)
- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Base Node
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Gateway WebSocket Clients 
- Relevant config (redacted): Base Gateway Configuration (`auth.mode = "none"/"token"`)

### Steps

1. Attach an operator to the target `sessions.messages.subscribe` endpoint. 
2. Actively execute `sessions.delete` or `sessions.reset` natively as an `operator.admin` against the tracked target.
3. Validate deterministic receipt of the `socket.drain` telemetry sequence via the local connection buffer. 

### Expected

- `socket.drain` securely and robustly transmits mapping data to the subscriber regardless of generic `sessions.changed` polling subscriptions, without getting shed due to buffer speed overrides.

### Actual

- The telemetry payload flows beautifully without throwing. 

## Evidence

Attach at least one:
- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Ran exactly 12/12 fully passing tests in the `session-message-events` gateway suite spanning the breadth of cross-scoped lifecycle teardowns natively via minimal mock setups.
- Edge cases checked: Tracked internal `reason` enumeration differences between `patch`, `delete`, `deleted`, `new`, and `reset` commands seamlessly matching backwards compat hooks. 
- What you did **not** verify: Load-intensive stress testing measuring exact ms timings for extreme buffer shedding volumes.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk**: Slight overhead mapped across connections retrieving identical updates if a subscriber has explicitly duplicated bindings into *both* events and messages endpoints parallel to each other.
  - **Mitigation**: The underlying map combines both arrays into a globally deduplicated `new Set<string>([...evSubs, ...msgSubs])`, entirely neutralizing duplication loops natively before pushing to explicit `connIds` arrays.

PR surface: Source +349, Tests +345, Docs +10. Total +704 across 5 files.

target.title: feat(doctor): detect local rebuild vs pristine npm release
target.body:
## Problem

A stock `openclaw update` silently overwrites locally patched installs.
Regressions return quietly because operators have no signal that the
running package has diverged from the pristine npm release.

Concrete trigger: the 2026-04-20 dist-overlay audit of
`/usr/lib/node_modules/openclaw` on a production host showed
v2026.4.15 with `dist/build-info.json.builtAt = 2026-04-19` while the
npm registry reported that version was published on `2026-04-16`.
That ~3-day skew is a locally rebuilt install — a stock `update` had
wiped a sticky-fallback patch that had been applied by hand.

## Solution

Non-blocking `openclaw doctor` note that compares local build metadata
against the npm registry publish time and warns if the installed
package is a local rebuild, an unreleased version, or has corrupt
metadata.

* **Warn only.** Never fails doctor.
* **Silent on pristine or inconclusive** (no network, no cache, etc.).
* **Runs in the install-posture section** of `doctor`, right after
  `noteSourceInstallIssues`.

## Signals

* Reads `<packageRoot>/dist/build-info.json` → `{version, commit, builtAt}`
  and `<packageRoot>/package.json.version`.
* Fetches `https://registry.npmjs.org/openclaw`, inspects
  `.time[<version>]`.
* **rebuild** — `builtAt - publishedAt >= 60 min`.
* **unreleased** — version not present in registry `.time`.
* **corrupt** — local `build-info.json` / `package.json` missing or
  malformed.
* **inconclusive** — registry fetch failed and no usable cache
  (silent, to avoid false positives on air-gapped installs).
* **pristine** — otherwise.

Registry response cached at `~/.openclaw/rebuild-guard-cache.json`
with a 6h TTL. Honors `OPENCLAW_NPM_REGISTRY_FILE` for tests and an
`offline: true` option for environments that should never hit the
network.

## Test coverage

`src/commands/doctor-rebuild-guard.test.ts` (8/8 green):

* pristine within the skew window
* rebuild when `builtAt` is >= 60 min after npm publish
* unreleased (version not in registry)
* corrupt (missing / malformed `build-info.json`)
* inconclusive (fetch throws, no cache) → silent
* offline mode (no network call, decides from local only)
* cache hit (second call, within TTL, does not call `fetch`)
* `noteRebuildGuardHealth` emission matrix: emits for
  `rebuild` / `unreleased` / `corrupt`; silent for
  `pristine` / `inconclusive`

## Gates

* `pnpm lint` — pass
* `pnpm tsgo:all` (typecheck) — pass
* `pnpm build` — pass
* `pnpm exec vitest run src/commands/doctor-rebuild-guard.test.ts` — 8/8

## Sample doctor output (rebuilt host)

```
└ Install ────────────────────────────────────────
  Local OpenClaw install differs from the pristine npm release.
  - Install: /usr/lib/node_modules/openclaw
  - Version: 2026.4.15
  - Commit: 8abd1fc
  - builtAt: 2026-04-19T08:42:11Z
  - npm publish: 2026-04-16T19:03:47Z
  - Skew: 4197 minutes ahead of npm
  - Reason: rebuild-after-publish
  Confirm every local patch has a matching upstream PR before running `openclaw update`.
```

## Related

A companion **Layer-1 bash guard** already ships in operator
workspaces (installed as a systemd `ExecStartPre`) to surface the same
warning on daemon start. This PR makes the same check native to every
`openclaw` install so end users benefit from it too, not just
operators who maintain local workspaces.

target.title: add option for MCP apps support
target.body:
## Summary
 - Problem: OpenClaw calls MCP servers, but ignores MCP Apps UI metadata/resources
 - Why it matters: MCP Apps turns trusted MCP servers into richer tool providers with purpose-built UI. Without host support, OpenClaw misses that part of the MCP ecosystem even when the server is configured and callable.
- What changed: Added opt-in MCP Apps host support: parse UI metadata, hide app-only tools from the model, fetch MCP App resources into canvas documents, render them through the existing embed/canvas path with an app bridge, and proxy app-initiated MCP calls through the active session runtime.
- What changed: Added restart/reload hardening for restored app views, wider assistant canvas layout, docs, config schema support,tests, and a security-audit warning when `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
-  What did NOT change (scope boundary): Normal MCP tool execution still works without MCP Apps. MCP Apps are default-off behind `mcp.apps.enabled`; this does not add a marketplace, new MCP server examples, or plugin-only architecture.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)
- MCP app-only tools are not materialized for the model.
- MCP Apps UI payloads are only fetched/rendered when `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
- App bridge calls are rejected when MCP Apps are disabled.
- App bridge calls enforce app-callable tool visibility.
- Persisted/restored app views can recreate an MCP runtime after gateway restart.
- Existing `[embed]` canvas rendering remains the fallback path when MCP Apps metadata is absent or disabled.

- Target test or file:
  - New opt-in config: `mcp.apps.enabled`.
  - Default behavior is unchanged for normal MCP tools; MCP Apps UI rendering is disabled by default.
  - When enabled, trusted MCP servers can render interactive app views in chat using the existing canvas/embed path.
  - MCP Apps views may call app-visible MCP tools through the active session runtime.
  - App-only MCP tools are hidden from the model.
  - `openclaw security audit` now warns when MCP Apps are enabled.
  - Docs now describe MCP Apps setup, scope, and security considerations. 
- Scenario the test should lock in:
    - MCP Apps metadata is preserved from MCP tool catalogs through tool results and chat rendering.
    - App-only tools are hidden from the model.
    - Model-only tools are blocked from app bridge calls.
    - MCP Apps UI rendering and bridge proxy calls require `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
    - Restored app views can recreate an MCP runtime after gateway restart.
    - Existing canvas/embed rendering remains the fallback when MCP Apps are disabled or metadata is absent.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:
    - The feature crosses runtime materialization, gateway proxying, config/bootstrap, and UI rendering. Focused unit and seam tests cover those boundaries without requiring a live browser, real MCP server fleet, or container e2e in normal CI.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any):
    - Existing canvas/embed tests covered generic iframe rendering, but not MCP Apps metadata, app-only visibility, app bridge calls, or restored MCP runtime behavior.

  ## User-visible / Behavior Changes
  - New opt-in config: `mcp.apps.enabled`.
  - Default behavior is unchanged for normal MCP tools; MCP Apps UI rendering is disabled by default.
  - When enabled, trusted MCP servers can render interactive app views in chat using the existing canvas/embed path.
  - MCP Apps views may call app-visible MCP tools through the active session runtime.
  - App-only MCP tools are hidden from the model.
  - `openclaw security audit` now warns when MCP Apps are enabled.
  - Docs now describe MCP Apps setup, scope, and security considerations.
  
## Diagram (if applicable)

  ```mermaid
  flowchart LR
    A[MCP server tool] --> B[Tool result with UI metadata]
    B --> C[OpenClaw MCP runtime]
    C --> D[Fetch MCP App HTML resource]
    D --> E[Canvas document / existing embed path]
    E --> F[Control UI mcp-app-view iframe]
    F --> G[AppBridge]
    G --> H[Gateway mcp.* proxy]
    H --> C
  ```
## Security Impact (required)
  - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): Yes
  - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
  - New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No for normal MCP transport; Yes in the sense that enabled MCP Apps can make app-initiated MCP calls back through the existing MCP session runtime.
  - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes
  - Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes
  - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
    - Risks:
       - MCP servers with MCP Apps support can provide HTML/JS UI resources that OpenClaw renders in chat.
       -  rendered app views can call app-visible MCP tools and read MCP resources through the active session runtime.
       - app views receive the tool input/result payload they are rendering.
    - Mitigations:
        - MCP Apps are default-off behind `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
        - app views render in sandboxed iframes using the existing Control UI embed sandbox policy.
        - app-only tools are hidden from the model, and model-only tools are blocked from app bridge calls.
        - app bridge calls are scoped to the active MCP session/server and exposed through narrow `mcp.*` gateway methods.
        - `openclaw security audit` warns when MCP Apps are enabled.
- Trust model: enabling MCP Apps means trusting the configured MCP servers not only as tool providers, but also as UI code providers.

  ## Repro + Verification

  ### Environment
  - OS: macOS
  - Runtime/container: local OpenClaw container via Podman
  - Model/provider: Claude Sonnet 4.6
  - Integration/channel (if any): Control UI chat with configured MCP servers
  - Relevant config (redacted):

```json
  {
    "mcp": {
      "apps": {
        "enabled": true
      },
      "servers": {
        "system-monitor": {
          "command": "npx",
          "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-system-monitor", "--stdio"]
        },
        "pdf": {
          "command": "npx",
          "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-pdf", "--stdio"]
        },
        "qr": {
          "url": "http://127.0.0.1:3108/mcp",
          "transport": "streamable-http"
        }
      }
    }
  }
```

### Steps
  1. Build and run an OpenClaw image from this branch.
  2. Enable MCP Apps with openclaw config set mcp.apps.enabled true --strict-json.
  3. Configure the system-monitor, pdf, and qr MCP servers.
  4. Start the QR MCP sidecar if using the QR example.
  5. Start a fresh chat/session in the Control UI.
  6. Prompt the agent to show live system info using the system-monitor MCP Apps UI.
  7. Prompt the agent to generate a QR code using the QR MCP Apps UI.
  8. Prompt the agent to display https://arxiv.org/pdf/1706.03762 using the PDF MCP Apps UI.
  9. Restart the OpenClaw container and reload the Control UI.
  10. Confirm the persisted PDF app view reloads and reconnects.

### Expected

  - Normal MCP tools still work.
  - With mcp.apps.enabled=true, MCP Apps UI resources render as interactive embedded app views.
  - System monitor renders a live monitor UI.
  - QR renders an embedded QR UI.
  - PDF renders an interactive PDF viewer.
  - Restored app views can reconnect after gateway/container restart.
  - With mcp.apps.enabled=false, app bridge calls are rejected and MCP Apps render through the normal fallback path instead of the app bridge.

### Actual

  - Verified locally with system-monitor, QR, and PDF MCP Apps examples.
  - Verified upstream image comparison: upstream can call the MCP servers but does not render the MCP Apps UI resources.
  - Verified restart/reload behavior for the PDF app view.

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/aaa93975-4406-493e-a060-4ac64763704b

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:
    - No migration is required for existing installs. MCP Apps support is default-off.
    - To enable it, set:
      ```bash
      openclaw config set mcp.apps.enabled true --strict-json
      ```
    - Configure MCP servers that expose MCP Apps / `mcp-ui` resources.
    - Restart OpenClaw so the updated config is loaded.

## Risks and Mitigations

  - Risk: Enabling MCP Apps allows configured MCP servers to provide HTML/JS UI that renders inside OpenClaw.
    - Mitigation: Feature is opt-in via `mcp.apps.enabled`; app UI renders in a sandboxed iframe; docs and security audit call out the trust boundary.
  - Risk: App views can call app-visible MCP tools/resources through the bridge, increasing the practical surface area of a trusted MCP server.
    - Mitigation: Bridge is scoped to MCP app methods only; model-only tools are blocked from app bridge calls; app-only tools are hidden from the model.
  - Risk: Restored chat views may need to reconnect to MCP runtimes after a container restart.
    - Mitigation: Gateway proxy lazily recreates the session MCP runtime from config when an app bridge call arrives and no runtime is currently active.
  - Risk: Servers without MCP Apps support, or installs with the feature disabled, should not regress.
    - Mitigation: Existing MCP tool execution path remains unchanged; UI falls back to the existing embed/canvas rendering behavior when MCP Apps are disabled or metadata is absent.

target.title: fix: normalize visible assistant output before delivery
target.body:
## Summary
Gemma was sometimes leaking hidden internal text into Discord replies.
This PR makes OpenClaw clean the final reply in one shared place before sending it.
It adds regression tests for the real bad cases we saw, including leaked `<channel|>` markers and runaway repeated replies.
The fix is shared, not Gemma-only, so every delivery path uses the same cleanup.


## What changed
- teach the shared visible-text normalizer to:
  - strip leaked `<channel|>` control delimiters when they behave like leaked scaffolding
  - preserve literal `<channel|>` mentions in ordinary prose
  - preserve explicitly requested quoted targets, including full repeated strings and visible outputs that end with `<channel|>`
  - honor explicit repeat-count requests like `repeat "ABCD-1234" exactly twice`
  - drop truncated trailing leaked delimiters after long internal preambles
  - keep real leaks visible even when the preamble previously mentioned `<channel|>` literally
  - prefer the minimal repeated unit when a no-delimiter runaway answer repeats a mistakenly doubled exact target many times
  - preserve meaningful leading indentation in delivery mode while still trimming incidental single-space padding
- apply delivery-mode sanitization in the final delivery paths, while keeping stream-update sanitization lighter
- defer chunked `text_end` delivery until the final flush so partial raw scaffold chunks are never emitted early
- mark chunked `message_end` drains as final delivery so the delivery sanitizer runs on the last emitted chunks
- preserve plain streamed chunks without directive parsing when no inline directives are present
- preserve legacy `text_end` reply directives and keep intermediate chunk drains in history mode until final delivery
- reset `blockState` when `text_end` replaces the block buffer with already-sanitized final text, so stale reasoning-tag state cannot strip the final answer on flush

## Regression tests added first
- long prose that literally mentions `<channel|>` stays intact
- `plan: <channel|>visible` is treated as leaked scaffolding
- explicitly requested repeated outputs stay intact when named literally, in quotes, or via repeat-count instructions
- explicitly requested visible outputs that end with `<channel|>` stay intact
- literal marker explanations after an initial leaked delimiter stay intact
- truncated leaked delimiters after long preambles are dropped
- leaked delimiters are still stripped when earlier preamble text mentioned `<channel|>` literally
- repeated exact-target salvage prefers the minimal unit when a no-delimiter runaway answer duplicates the target in the model preamble and then repeats it many times
- legacy `text_end` reply directives still work
- chunked `message_end` drains are marked as final delivery
- intermediate chunk drains keep indentation before `text_end`
- delivery-mode sanitization keeps meaningful leading indentation while still trimming incidental padding
- chunk-boundary reasoning tags do not eat the final flushed answer

## Verification
- failing regressions were added first and reproduced locally before the fixes
- focused tests:
  - `pnpm exec vitest run src/shared/text/repeated-visible-suffix.test.ts src/shared/text/assistant-visible-text.test.ts`
  - `pnpm exec vitest run src/shared/text/repeated-visible-suffix.test.ts src/shared/text/assistant-visible-text.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-utils.strip-model-special-tokens.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.messages.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.reply-tags.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-pi-session.emits-block-replies-text-end-does-not.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-pi-session.keeps-indented-fenced-blocks-intact.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/reply-utils.test.ts`
- `pnpm build`
- live Discord probe on `vllm/gemma4-e4b` after restarting the local gateway on this branch:
  - Looper prompt: `reply with exactly pr68986-live2-1776657698 and nothing else`
  - Bob visible Discord reply: `pr68986-live2-1776657698`

target.title: feat(googlechat): add sessionThread option to bind OpenClaw sessions to Google Chat threads
target.body: AI-assisted: yes (Claude Opus 4.7). ## Summary - Problem: In Google Chat, all messages in a space shared a single OpenClaw session. Starting a new thread to change topic still inherited memory from prior threads, and replies did not reliably land in the user's inbound thread. - Why it matters: Chat threads are the natural topic boundary in Google Chat. Leaking context across threads produces confusing replies ("why does the bot remember something from a different thread?"), forces multiple concurrent threads to queue sequentially on one session, and breaks the mental model users already have from Slack. Requested in #13373. - What changed: Added an opt-in `sessionThread` channel option. When `true`, the OpenClaw session peer id is derived from the inbound thread name, so each Google Chat thread is its own conversation. Replies are now threaded via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so bot responses land in the user's thread. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Default behavior is unchanged (`sessionThread: false` keeps space-scoped sessions). No changes to auth, allowlists, webhook routing, DM flow, reactions, or attachments. No slash commands / `/focus`-style bindings added (see "future work" below). ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #13373 (per-thread session mode feature request) - Closes #41788 (Google Chat replies creating new threads instead of staying in the existing one) - Related #44347 — resolves the first of the two requests in that issue - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ### Note on naming vs the issue's proposal The issue proposes `threadSessionMode: "per-thread" | "per-space"`. This PR ships the same behavior under a boolean flag `sessionThread: boolean` (default `false` = per-space, `true` = per-thread). Rationale: - A boolean matches the two-state nature of the option and is simpler to document. - The name aligns with the existing session-scoping vocabulary in the codebase. - Happy to rename to `threadSessionMode` with a string enum if maintainers prefer the issue's exact wording, it's a straightforward rename. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: The Google Chat channel used the space id as the session peer id and did not pass the inbound thread name back on outbound messages. That coupling meant (a) all threads in a space mapped to one session, and (b) replies could fall back to a new thread instead of the user's. - Missing detection / guardrail: No peer-id unit coverage for Google Chat; Slack already has `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` as prior art for this exact pattern, but nothing equivalent existed for Google Chat. - Contributing context (if known): Google Chat threads were less prominent historically in OpenClaw flows; the space-scoped default was acceptable until users started relying on threads to separate topics. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: `extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` returns the thread name when `sessionThread` is enabled and an inbound thread is present; falls back to `spaceId` otherwise; and produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions do not cross. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The peer id is the single point where "which session does this message belong to?" is decided for Google Chat. Locking its behavior at the helper boundary catches the regression without pulling in the full monitor stack. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): None for Google Chat. Slack's `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` covers the analogous primitive. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - New opt-in config: `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` (default `false`). - `false` (default): space-scoped sessions, prior behavior unchanged. - `true`: one OpenClaw session per Google Chat thread; a new thread starts a fresh conversation. - Outbound messages now consistently target the inbound thread via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so replies land in the user's thread more reliably. This applies regardless of `sessionThread`. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text sessionThread: false (default, unchanged) space S, thread A -> session(peer=S) space S, thread B -> session(peer=S) # shared memory sessionThread: true space S, thread A -> session(peer=A) space S, thread B -> session(peer=B) # isolated memory space S, no thread -> session(peer=S) # falls back to space-scoped ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - New/changed network calls? No (same Chat API endpoints; only request payload now sets `thread.name` and `messageReplyOption`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? No - Data access scope changed? No (in fact, `sessionThread: true` narrows memory scope per thread) ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: Node 22 / local gateway (`pnpm openclaw gateway restart`) - Model/provider: any (provider-agnostic) - Integration/channel (if any): Google Chat - Relevant config (redacted): ```jsonc { "channels": { "googlechat": { "enabled": true, "serviceAccountFile": "/path/to/service-account.json", "sessionThread": true } } } ``` ### Steps 1. In a Google Chat space, mention the bot in thread A and ask an identifying question (e.g. "remember the color red"). 2. Start a new thread B in the same space and ask "what color did I mention?". 3. Reply again inside thread A and ask the same question. ### Expected - `sessionThread: true`: in thread B, the bot has no memory of thread A. In thread A, the bot still remembers "red". Replies land in the thread the user messaged in. - `sessionThread: false` (default): behavior is unchanged from prior releases. ### Actual - Matches expected for both modes. ## Evidence - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after ``` pnpm test extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is disabled ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is undefined (default) ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns thread name when sessionThread is enabled and inbound has a thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId falls back to spaceId when sessionThread is enabled but inbound has no thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions don't cross Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 5 passed (5) ``` - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - When disabled(previous behavior): - When enabled: - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) - Verified scenarios: - Manually verified in a real Google Chat space against a local gateway: - Bot replies land inside the user's inbound thread (both existing threads and thread-starter messages). - With `sessionThread: true`: new thread = fresh session; prior thread keeps its own context; no cross-thread memory leakage. - With `sessionThread: false`: unchanged, space-scoped memory. - Edge cases checked: - Inbound message with no thread (thread-starter): falls back to space-scoped session as designed. - Two concurrent threads in the same space maintain separate sessions. - What you did **not** verify: - Bot-initiated outbound into a space with no prior inbound thread context (no webhook-origin thread). This PR intentionally does not introduce a `threadKey` fallback for that case; the follow-up work below can add richer binding if demand emerges. - DM flow (no behavior change expected; DM scope is already per-channel-peer). ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? Yes - Config/env changes? Yes, additive only: new optional `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` boolean (default `false`). - Migration needed? No. Existing configs continue to behave as before. - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A. To opt in, set `channels.googlechat.sessionThread: true`. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: Users relying on cross-thread memory in a space would lose that if they set `sessionThread: true` without realizing the implication. - Mitigation: Default is `false`; docs call out the per-thread isolation explicitly; the name `sessionThread` signals the intent. - Risk: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` is a new exported helper surface from the plugin's `monitor.ts`. - Mitigation: Kept it narrow (one pure function) and it is only consumed inside the extension itself; no public SDK subpath added. ## Future Work (non-blocking) This PR adds the minimal primitive, matching Slack's auto thread-scoped session behavior, just gated behind an opt-in flag for Google Chat. A follow-up could align with the Telegram/Discord `session.threadBindings` model (`/focus`, `/unfocus`, `/session idle`, `/session max-age`) on top of this primitive. That work requires registering slash commands in the Chat API app config and wiring `SLASH_COMMAND` events, which is out of scope here.

target.title: fix(acp): treat missing cwd as stale bound session
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: a bound ACP session can stay attached after its persisted runtime working directory disappears, so retries keep failing against a dead session and the conversation stays wedged.
- Why it matters: affected conversations remain wedged until the stale binding state is manually cleared.
- What changed: during ACP dispatch, treat a generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` as stale when the bound session's persisted `runtimeOptions.cwd` can no longer be resolved on disk (`ENOENT`/`ENOTDIR`), then unbind the dead conversation. The signal is folded into the existing `isStaleSessionInitError` classifier and the existing `maybeUnbindStaleBoundConversations` dispatch helper — no new module, no parallel helper.
- Scope: dispatch-only. Two files: `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts` and its test.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- ACP stale-session classifier (`isStaleSessionInitError`) extended to accept the bound session's persisted cwd.
- Auto-reply ACP dispatch stale-binding cleanup (`maybeUnbindStaleBoundConversations`) passes the persisted cwd at the `runTurn` catch.
- Regression tests: generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` + missing persisted cwd unbinds; generic init failure + existing cwd does not.

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #60720
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

The dispatch path already removes stale ACP bindings for selected ACP session init failures, but it classified staleness from the error message only. The live missing-cwd case surfaces as a generic spawn `ENOENT` wrapped as `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`, whose message does not match the stale-message set. The reliable signal is the bound session's persisted `runtimeOptions.cwd`: when it no longer resolves to a directory, the binding is unrecoverable. Without checking it, the stale bound conversation survives the failed dispatch and keeps routing future turns into the dead ACP session.

## Implementation Notes

- Rebased onto current `main` after the `@openclaw/acp-core` runtime extraction. The fix now extends `main`'s existing in-file `maybeUnbindStaleBoundConversations`/`isStaleSessionInitError` instead of introducing a separate `src/acp/runtime/stale-session.ts` helper (which no longer fits the post-extraction layout). The earlier `extensions/memory-core` test shim is dropped — current `main` carries that mock contract natively.
- Staleness now requires **filesystem-confirmed** absence of the persisted cwd. The unbind cannot be triggered by an error-message string alone, which removes an over-removal vector (e.g. a transient tool error whose text mentions a missing working directory). `ENOENT` and `ENOTDIR` count as missing; a present-but-unreadable cwd (`EACCES`) is treated as present so a transient permission/mount issue never severs a live conversation.

## Maintainer Decision (session-state semantics)

This removes persisted bound-conversation state when a generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` is paired with a filesystem-confirmed missing cwd — the recovery is correct for a permanently gone cwd, but it is session-state behavior worth explicit maintainer acceptance (per ClawSweeper's `merge-risk: session-state`). Two residual, deliberately out-of-scope behaviors to weigh:

- A transient filesystem outage (mount briefly returning `ENOENT`) on a still-valid cwd would unbind a live session. Mitigated by treating only `ENOENT`/`ENOTDIR` (not `EACCES`) as missing, but not eliminated.
- `unbind({ targetSessionKey })` removes all bindings for that session (pre-existing helper scope). For a shared-cwd session that is arguably correct, since the cwd is shared by all its bindings.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: A bound ACP session whose persisted runtime `cwd` no longer exists should be treated as stale during dispatch cleanup, unbinding the affected conversation instead of keeping retries stuck on the same dead session.
- Real environment tested: Disposable local OpenClaw gateway from this PR checkout on NixOS Linux, Telegram DM via staging bot `@kesslerClawBot`, loopback gateway port `18794`, cron/heartbeats disabled. Gateway was stopped after the proof run.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started the disposable staging gateway from PR head `1ad568ba1e0db9051904eacee025ee36ec2f06ee`, sent a Telegram DM to `@kesslerClawBot` that routed through a bound ACP conversation whose persisted `runtimeOptions.cwd` pointed at `/home/art/.local/state/openclaw-staging/disposable/missing-cwd-pr68843`, let ACP dispatch fail with generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`, then inspected the post-run binding state.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Redacted public proof artifact: https://gist.github.com/kesslerio/dbefdac0c5d5a5849997b6bf702047c6. Follow-up proof comment: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/68843#issuecomment-4558022819.

  ```txt
  2026-05-27T12:18:57.491-07:00 telegram: routed via bound conversation 8352721935 -> agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1
  2026-05-27T12:18:57.520-07:00 telegram inbound: chatId=8352721935 ... preview="[...] PR68843 patched missing cwd cleanup proof"
  2026-05-27T12:19:06.365-07:00 [plugins] embedded acpx runtime backend ready
  2026-05-27T12:19:06.400-07:00 dispatch-acp: removed 1 stale bound conversation(s) for agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1 after ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED: Failed to spawn agent command: /run/current-system/sw/bin/codex-acp-openclaw
  2026-05-27T12:19:06.404-07:00 acp-dispatch: session=agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1 outcome=error code=ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED latencyMs=327 queueDepth=0 activeRuntimes=0
  ```

  Post-run binding state:

  ```json
  {
    "bindingCount": 0,
    "sessionKey": "agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1",
    "acpState": "error",
    "runtimeOptions": {
      "cwd": "/home/art/.local/state/openclaw-staging/disposable/missing-cwd-pr68843"
    }
  }
  ```

- Observed result after fix: the staging Telegram DM routed through the stale bound ACP conversation, ACPX reached the runtime backend, dispatch failed with generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`, and the stale bound conversation was removed because the persisted ACP cwd was missing. Post-run binding state showed `bindingCount: 0`.
- What was not tested: Production AlphaClaw was not used. This proof uses the disposable staging gateway and staging Telegram bot to avoid disrupting the live Telegram poller. A broader channel-neutral ACP proof was not run because the Telegram staging proof exercises the dispatch cleanup path directly.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the raw local `/home/art/...` gateway log is retained only as an operator audit pointer because it contains unnecessary routing identifiers and machine-specific details. Public proof is the redacted gist and copied transcript above.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): current `main` only treated selected ACP init-failure messages as stale and did not inspect persisted ACP session `cwd` metadata for a generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`; ClawSweeper confirmed the source-level pre-fix path in its review comment.

## Tests and validation

Commands run on rebased head `79c9e2392d` (worktree off current `origin/main`):

```sh
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts -t "stale|cwd"
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
```

Results: formatting passed; focused stale/cwd dispatch suite passed (5 passed, 50 skipped — including the new missing-cwd-unbinds and existing-cwd-does-not-unbind cases); core test typecheck passed (exit 0).

## Security Impact

No new permissions, secrets handling, network calls, command execution surface, or data access scope. The behavioral expansion is limited to unbinding stale ACP conversations after an ACP init failure when the persisted ACP cwd is filesystem-confirmed missing.

## Compatibility / Migration

No config or migration required. The change affects only stale ACP init failure recovery.

## AI Assistance

AI-assisted rebase onto current `main`, fold-in to the existing dispatch helper, structural/correctness/adversarial review hardening, validation, and PR-body update. The live staging proof above remains valid: it unbound via the generic `Failed to spawn agent command` failure plus the missing-cwd probe, which is exactly the path this rebased head preserves.

target.title: fix(telegram): reject zero and negative replyToMessageId values
target.body:
## Summary

- `normalizeTelegramReplyToMessageId` now rejects `0` and negative values in addition to non-numeric inputs
- Telegram message IDs are always positive integers; passing `0` or a negative value would result in a `400 Bad Request` from the Telegram API
- Both the numeric branch (`typeof value === "number"`) and the string branch now enforce `> 0`

## Test plan

- [ ] `normalizeTelegramReplyToMessageId(0)` returns `undefined`
- [ ] `normalizeTelegramReplyToMessageId(-1)` returns `undefined`
- [ ] `normalizeTelegramReplyToMessageId("0")` returns `undefined`
- [ ] `normalizeTelegramReplyToMessageId("-5")` returns `undefined`
- [ ] `normalizeTelegramReplyToMessageId(42)` returns `42`
- [ ] `normalizeTelegramReplyToMessageId("99")` returns `99`
- [ ] UUID strings return `undefined`
- [ ] Existing `parseTelegramThreadId` behaviour is unchanged
