target.title: fix(memory): preserve surrogate pairs in chunker; sanitize embed inputs
target.body:
## Summary

- Fix the outer coarse-split loop in `chunkMarkdown` so it does not bisect a UTF-16 surrogate pair (root cause of indexing stalls).
- Add a defensive `stripUnpairedSurrogates` sanitizer at the embed boundary (the safety net #27753 originally requested).
- Tests for both, including a new regression test that triggers the exact code path the existing surrogate-pair test missed.

Closes #27753.
Fixes #65782.

## Why

Memory indexing stalls permanently for any agent whose content contains an emoji or supplementary-plane character that the chunker happens to split across a surrogate-pair boundary. `openclaw memory status` reports the index as `clean / no issues` while ~98% of sessions are unindexed.

End-to-end symptom (verbose):

```
[memory] embeddings: batch start  (×N)
Memory index failed (<agent>): openai embeddings failed: 500
{"error":{"message":"... 'utf-8' codec can't encode character '\\ud83c'
in position 3229: surrogates not allowed ..."}}
```

Two failure modes compound:

1. **Chunker** (`packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/internal.ts`) — the inner fine-split loop already guards against splitting inside a surrogate pair, but the outer coarse loop advances by `start += maxChars` without checking the boundary. When the cut lands inside e.g. an emoji like 🌸 (`U+1F338` = `\uD83C\uDF38`), the chunk ends with a lone high surrogate and the next begins with a lone low surrogate. The existing surrogate-pair test (`"does not break surrogate pairs when splitting long CJK lines"`) does not catch this because it uses an all-surrogate-pair line — the high `estimateStringChars` weight forces the inner pass, which is correct.
2. **Embedding HTTP path** — Python JSON encoders (e.g. via LiteLLM proxies) reject lone surrogates with a 500 wrapping a `UnicodeEncodeError`. The retry policy only matches `rate_limit | 429 | 5\d\d | cloudflare | tokens per day`, but the body shape is a connect-error, not a transient one — and the indexer is transactional, so a single bad batch rolls back the entire run. Every subsequent `openclaw memory index` invocation reproduces the same failure.

## What changed

### `packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/internal.ts`

```diff
- for (let start = 0; start < line.length; start += maxChars) {
-   const coarse = line.slice(start, start + maxChars);
+ for (let start = 0; start < line.length; ) {
+   let coarseEnd = Math.min(line.length, start + maxChars);
+   // Avoid splitting inside a UTF-16 surrogate pair (emoji, CJK Extension B+).
+   if (coarseEnd < line.length) {
+     const lastCode = line.charCodeAt(coarseEnd - 1);
+     if (lastCode >= 0xd800 && lastCode <= 0xdbff) {
+       coarseEnd += 1; // include the trailing low surrogate
+     }
+   }
+   const coarse = line.slice(start, coarseEnd);
+   start = coarseEnd;
```

Mirrors the existing inner-loop guard at the same file/line. New regression test (`"does not break surrogate pairs at the coarse split boundary (issue #27753)"`) constructs a line of `39 ASCII + 🌸 + 39 ASCII` so the outer cut at `start + maxChars = 40` lands between the high and low surrogate. Without the fix, the test fails by producing chunks containing lone surrogates.

### `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-embedding-ops.ts`

Adds a tiny exported helper:

```ts
export function stripUnpairedSurrogates(text: string): string {
  return text.replace(
    /[\uD800-\uDBFF](?![\uDC00-\uDFFF])|(?<![\uD800-\uDBFF])[\uDC00-\uDFFF]/g,
    "\uFFFD",
  );
}
```

…and applies it at the entry of `embedBatchWithRetry` and `embedBatchInputsWithRetry`. This is the change #27753 originally requested. It protects against any other code path that might produce a lone surrogate (LLM tool output, file ingestion, future chunkers) — chunker-only fix is necessary but not sufficient.

Adds `manager-embedding-ops.test.ts` with focused unit tests for the helper (preserves valid pairs, replaces lone high/low surrogates, handles reversed pairs, no-op on empty/non-string).

## Test plan

- [x] Verified live on installed runtime (`openclaw 2026.4.5`) by patching the equivalent bundled artifact under `dist/manager-CKYnEo0k.js` and running `openclaw memory index --agent <id>`:

  | | before | after |
  |---|---:|---:|
  | clawdea files | 6 | **166** |
  | clawdea chunks | 93 | **4584** |
  | dev files | 2 | **17** |
  | dev chunks | 251 | **588** |

  `openclaw memory search` works end-to-end after the fix.
- [ ] CI runs `pnpm test` against the new unit tests in `extensions/memory-core` and `packages/memory-host-sdk`.
- [ ] Existing surrogate-pair test (`"does not break surrogate pairs when splitting long CJK lines"`) still passes.

## Notes for reviewers

- I did not run `pnpm install` locally to execute vitest — the runtime fix is verified live, and the new tests follow the same vitest patterns as adjacent files (`manager-embedding-cache.test.ts`, `internal.test.ts`).
- The retry-policy gap (`fetch failed` / `UnicodeEncodeError` 500 not retried) is a separate concern tracked in #56815 / #58255 — out of scope for this PR. With the chunker + sanitizer in place, the original failure no longer reaches the retry path.
- If the team prefers the helper to live in a shared utility module (e.g. under `packages/memory-host-sdk`) rather than `manager-embedding-ops.ts`, happy to relocate.

target.title: feat(discord): add canvas-first Discord Activities support
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

If this PR fixes a plugin beta-release blocker, title it `fix(<plugin-id>): beta blocker - <summary>` and link the matching `Beta blocker: <plugin-name> - <summary>` issue labeled `beta-blocker`. Contributors cannot label PRs, so the title is the PR-side signal for maintainers and automation.

- Problem: Discord Activity launch/setup in OpenClaw required brittle manual component payloads, had weak agent guidance, and failed command deploys when Discord required retaining the Entry Point command.
- Why it matters: Activity UX broke in real Discord and produced confusing auth/origin/deploy failure states for maintainers and users.
- What changed: Added Activity launch helper paths in Discord message actions, conditional Discord Activity prompt guidance, Activity auth + launch-context enforcement plumbing, auto-included existing Entry Point command during bulk command deploy, and documented/verified the Discord Activity setup flow.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No new standalone backend OAuth exchange endpoint was added; OAuth exchange remains app/backend-owned.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix

- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

## Linked Issue/PR

- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- Root cause: Discord Activity launch assumptions diverged from Discord runtime behavior (root launch path + Entry Point command requirements), and OpenClaw lacked resilient deploy/request handling around those constraints.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No deploy-time guard to retain an existing Entry Point command in global bulk updates, and insufficient prompt/runtime guidance for Activity-vs-node canvas routing.
- Contributing context (if known): Discord iframe/origin constraints and launch metadata requirements were not first-class in the earlier flow.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test

target.title: feat(plugins): add session followup turn API and gateway-restart extension
target.body:
## Summary

Add a new `runtime.followup.enqueueFollowupTurn()` method to the plugin runtime, enabling plugins to schedule proactive agent turns for any session — including cold sessions with no active user interaction.

> **Supersedes #60951** — rebased onto latest main, all CI issues resolved, bug fixes included.

## Problem

Plugins have no way to inject a user-role message into an existing session and trigger an agent turn. The available primitives (`enqueueSystemEvent`, `subagent.run`) either don't initiate turns or create new sessions instead of continuing the original conversation. This blocks use cases like:

- **Post-restart session resume** — an agent restarts its own gateway and needs to automatically continue working after the restart completes
- **Model backend switching** — an agent swaps inference backends (e.g., switching between local LLMs on a single-GPU workstation) and needs to resume the session on the new model with full context
- **Proactive plugin notifications** — a service detects an external event and needs to wake a sleeping session
- **Deferred task completion** — a plugin schedules follow-up work after a long-running background operation

## Solution

### Core API: `runtime.followup.enqueueFollowupTurn()`

A new `followup` namespace on `PluginRuntimeCore` that:

1. **Reconstructs a full `FollowupRun`** from the session store via `buildFollowupRunForSession()` — resolving agent identity, workspace, provider/model, session file, and delivery context from the persisted `SessionEntry`.

2. **Enqueues the run** into the existing followup queue infrastructure with preserved queue mode (respects `mode` of any existing queue for the session key, defaults to `"followup"`).

3. **Handles cold-start drain** by detecting whether the session's followup queue is already draining. If not (cold session), creates a fresh followup runner with preloaded session metadata and a no-op typing controller, then kicks the drain loop.

```typescript
// Plugin usage
const enqueued = await api.runtime.followup.enqueueFollowupTurn({
  sessionKey: "agent:my-agent:telegram:direct:12345",
  prompt: "Background task complete. Here are the results...",
  source: "my-plugin",
});
```

### Bundled Extension: `gateway-restart`

A reference implementation that demonstrates the followup turn API:

- **Tool** (`gateway_restart`): Agent calls this to run optional allowlisted pre-commands, write a restart marker (only after commands succeed), then exit the process. Systemd (or equivalent) auto-restarts the gateway.

- **Service** (`gateway-restart-watcher`): On startup, checks for the restart marker. If found, calls `enqueueFollowupTurn()` to resume the original session with a completion message. Marker is deleted only after successful enqueue — preserved for retry on failure.

Disabled by default (`enabledByDefault: false`). Opt-in via `plugins.entries.gateway-restart.enabled: true`.

## Files Changed

| File | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `src/auto-reply/reply/queue/build-followup-run.ts` | New: cold-session FollowupRun builder with session store resolution |
| `src/plugins/runtime/runtime-followup.ts` | New: plugin runtime followup namespace with hot/cold session detection |
| `src/plugins/runtime/types-core.ts` | Add `followup` type to `PluginRuntimeCore` |
| `src/plugins/runtime/index.ts` | Wire `createRuntimeFollowup()` |
| `extensions/gateway-restart/index.ts` | New: gateway-restart plugin (tool + watcher service) |
| `extensions/gateway-restart/openclaw.plugin.json` | New: plugin manifest |
| `extensions/gateway-restart/package.json` | New: package metadata |
| `test/helpers/plugins/plugin-runtime-mock.ts` | Add `followup` to mock |
| `**/**.test.ts` | 24 unit tests across 3 test files |

## Bug Fixes (vs #60951)

1. **Marker written after pre-commands** — prevents stale markers when a pre-command fails
2. **Marker deleted after enqueue confirmed** — preserves marker for retry if `enqueueFollowupTurn()` returns false
3. **Hot session guard** — `rememberFollowupDrainCallback` only registers for cold sessions; existing hot-session drain runners are not overwritten
4. **Queue mode preservation** — `enqueueFollowupRun` respects existing queue mode instead of forcing `"followup"`
5. **Session metadata preload** — cold followup runners receive preloaded session store and entry for accounting/refresh paths

## Testing

- 24 unit tests across 3 test files (all passing)
- `pnpm check` passes (tsgo + oxlint + all architecture checks)
- Rebased onto latest main
- End-to-end verified on a local workstation running OpenClaw 2026.4.8 with Gemma 4 26B via llama.cpp

## Use Cases This Enables

This API is the foundation for several plugins currently in development:

- **`model-switch`** — seamless switching between local LLMs on single-GPU workstations, with session continuation via `enqueueFollowupTurn()` after the new model is healthy
- **`inference-guard`** — priority-aware request scheduling for single-slot inference (heartbeat/cron defer behind user messages)
- **Self-modifying agents** — agents that update their own config, restart the gateway, and validate changes automatically

## Test plan

- [ ] `pnpm check` passes
- [ ] `pnpm vitest run extensions/gateway-restart/index.test.ts` — 6 tests pass
- [ ] `pnpm vitest run src/plugins/runtime/runtime-followup.test.ts` — 9 tests pass
- [ ] `pnpm vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/queue/build-followup-run.test.ts` — 9 tests pass
- [ ] Gateway-restart tool writes marker only after pre-commands succeed
- [ ] Watcher service enqueues followup turn and deletes marker on success
- [ ] Watcher preserves marker on enqueue failure

## AI Disclosure

This PR was developed with AI assistance (Claude Code). Testing level: **fully tested** — 24 unit tests, `pnpm check` green, end-to-end verified on local hardware. The author understands all code in this PR and can explain design decisions.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: feat(plugins): upgrade llm_input/llm_output hooks from fire-and-forget to modifying
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `llm_input` and `llm_output` plugin hooks are fire-and-forget (observe-only). Plugins cannot block, modify, or redact LLM calls/responses.
- Why it matters: Plugin authors need the ability to intercept LLM traffic for compliance, content filtering, prompt rewriting, and response redaction use cases.
- What changed: Both hooks now run sequentially via `runModifyingHook`. `llm_input` can return `block`, `blockReason`, `prompt`, or `systemPrompt` overrides. `llm_output` can return replacement `assistantTexts`. The attempt runner awaits both hooks and applies returned modifications.
- Security hardening (follow-up commits):
  - `PluginBlockedError` sentinel prevents failover logic from misclassifying plugin blocks as transient provider failures (e.g. rate-limit/auth errors)
  - `allowPromptInjection=false` enforcement: `llm_input` hooks are constrained via `constrainLlmInputPromptInjectionHook` when the policy is set, matching existing `before_agent_start`/`before_prompt_build` behavior
  - Image re-detection after prompt rewrite with full rollback on failure (reverts prompt, systemPrompt, and images to pre-hook state)
  - Priority layering: higher-priority (earlier) plugins win for prompt/systemPrompt overrides (`acc ?? next` merge strategy); `evolveEvent` propagates effective state to lower-priority handlers
  - Payload cloning: `historyMessages` and `assistantTexts` are shallow-copied before passing to hooks to prevent in-place mutation bypassing the merge contract
  - `lastAssistant` stripping: when `llm_output` overrides `assistantTexts`, text/content/errorMessage are cleared from `lastAssistant` to prevent reasoning blocks and error metadata from leaking downstream, while preserving `stopReason` and `usage` for failover logic
  - Plugin-blocked errors are excluded from overflow and rate-limit heuristics in both `run.ts` and `agent-runner-execution.ts`
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): Hook event shapes are unchanged (additive result types only). Plugins that return `void` behave identically to before. No changes to other hooks, plugin discovery, or the Plugin SDK public surface.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #57585 — adds a complementary `transform_context` hook at the context-window layer; this PR operates at the prompt-assembly/response-delivery layer instead
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Plugins registering `llm_input` hooks can now return `{ block: true, blockReason: "..." }` to prevent an LLM call from being made.
- Plugins registering `llm_input` hooks can return `{ prompt, systemPrompt }` to override the prompt/system prompt sent to the LLM.
- Plugins registering `llm_output` hooks can return `{ assistantTexts: [...] }` to replace the assistant response (e.g. for redaction).
- Existing plugins that return `void` from these hooks are unaffected — fully backward compatible.
- When `allowPromptInjection=false` is set for a plugin, `llm_input` prompt/systemPrompt fields are stripped from the hook result (block/blockReason still honored).

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[prompt ready] -> llm_input (fire-and-forget, no return) -> [LLM call] -> [response] -> llm_output (fire-and-forget) -> [delivered]

After:
[prompt ready] -> await llm_input -> [block? -> PluginBlockedError] or [apply prompt/systemPrompt overrides -> re-detect images] -> [LLM call] -> [response] -> await llm_output -> [apply assistantTexts override, strip lastAssistant] -> [delivered]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: Plugins can now modify prompts and block/alter LLM responses. This is gated behind the existing plugin hook registration mechanism — only installed and enabled plugins can register these hooks. The blocking path throws a dedicated `PluginBlockedError` sentinel that is logged and excluded from failover/overflow misclassification. Prompt modifications are subject to `allowPromptInjection` policy enforcement. Hook payloads are cloned to prevent mutation. Response overrides strip original content from `lastAssistant` to prevent data leaks. Prompt/response modifications flow through the same trust boundary as existing modifying hooks (e.g. `before_tool_call`).

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22+ / Bun
- Model/provider: Any
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): Plugin with `llm_input`/`llm_output` hook registered

### Steps

1. Register a plugin with an `llm_input` hook that returns `{ block: true, blockReason: "test" }`
2. Trigger an agent run
3. Observe the LLM call is blocked with the logged reason

### Expected

- LLM call is blocked and error is thrown with reason

### Actual

- LLM call is blocked and error is thrown with reason

## Evidence

- [x] Code review hardening: all 12 review comments from automated reviewers (Codex/Greptile) addressed across 9 follow-up commits
- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets: manual gateway test with a local test plugin confirmed `llm_input` and `llm_output` hooks fire correctly on v2026.4.1 gateway. Logs show `llm_input fired` with prompt rewrite (`-> rewriting prompt (prepending tag)`), `llm_output fired` with `assistantTexts (1)` and usage data, and `-> BLOCKING: prompt contains "BLOCKED"` for the block path. The released gateway treats hooks as fire-and-forget (return values ignored), confirming backward compatibility; modifying behavior (block/rewrite/redact) is validated by 22 automated tests.
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Hook blocking, prompt override, response redaction via test plugins, multi-plugin priority chaining with `evolveEvent` propagation
- Manual gateway testing: installed a local test plugin (`llm-hook-test`) on a live v2026.4.1 gateway via `openclaw plugins install -l`. Confirmed hook registration, `llm_input` firing with prompt data (provider, model, sessionId, imagesCount), `llm_output` firing with assistantTexts and usage. Tested block, rewrite, redact, and observe scenarios through the dashboard web UI. Verified the released gateway's fire-and-forget path is unaffected (backward compat).
- Automated tests: 22/22 tests pass in `wired-hooks-llm-modifying.test.ts` covering block result, prompt/systemPrompt override, evolveEvent propagation, multi-plugin priority merge, backward compat (void returns), and error handling (graceful degradation).
- Edge cases checked: Plugin returning void (backward compat), hook failure graceful degradation (logged warning, continues), `allowPromptInjection=false` constraint, image re-detection failure rollback, empty prompt override (`prompt: ""`), `PluginBlockedError` bypassing failover/overflow heuristics
- What you did **not** verify: End-to-end production load with many concurrent plugins across multiple providers

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: A misbehaving plugin could block all LLM calls or silently rewrite prompts.
  - Mitigation: Blocking is logged with reason via dedicated `PluginBlockedError`. Prompt/response modifications are opt-in (plugin must explicitly return override fields). `allowPromptInjection=false` can constrain prompt modification. Hook payloads are cloned. Existing hook failure handling catches and logs errors gracefully without crashing the run. Higher-priority plugins take precedence via first-setter-wins merge strategy.

target.title: [codex] Update Claude CLI billing docs
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

OpenClaw's Anthropic provider docs still described the previously announced June 15, 2026 Claude Agent SDK / `claude -p` billing change as active. Anthropic's current support article now says that change is paused, so the old wording could mislead users about whether `claude -p` draws from a separate Agent SDK credit or from their subscription usage limits.

## Why This Change Was Made

This updates the Claude CLI billing guidance to match Anthropic's current public support language:

- Anthropic's Agent SDK plan article says the June 15 change is paused and that Claude Agent SDK, `claude -p`, and third-party app usage still draw from subscription usage limits for now: https://support.claude.com/en/articles/15036540-use-the-claude-agent-sdk-with-your-claude-plan
- Anthropic's Pro/Max Claude Code article documents subscription-backed Claude Code usage and plan-limit behavior: https://support.claude.com/en/articles/11145838-use-claude-code-with-your-pro-or-max-plan
- Anthropic's Team/Enterprise Claude Code article documents Team and Enterprise subscription behavior: https://support.claude.com/en/articles/11845131-use-claude-code-with-your-team-or-enterprise-plan
- Claude Code's CLI reference still documents `claude -p` as SDK-style non-interactive usage: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/cli-reference

The docs now point users back to Anthropic's support articles before they rely on subscription billing behavior.

## User Impact

Users evaluating Anthropic API keys, Claude CLI reuse, or Claude subscription proxy workflows get current billing guidance and direct official references. The recommendation to use API keys for predictable production or shared automation remains unchanged.

## Evidence

- `corepack pnpm docs:list`
- `corepack pnpm format:docs:check`
- `corepack pnpm docs:check-mdx`
- `git diff --check`
- `rg -n "Starting June 15|monthly Agent SDK credit first|no longer draws from normal Claude|separate monthly Agent SDK credit" docs -S` returned no matches

target.title: fix(sessions-files): exclude workspace-external files from workspace rail list
target.body:
Fixes #95685

## What Problem This Solves

The Session Workspace Rail (right sidebar, added in 2026.6.9) marks files outside the configured workspace directory as "missing" (red badge), even though those files exist and were successfully read during the session. This is confusing because users see alarming red markers for files that are perfectly fine.

## Evidence

### Fix

In `buildListResult()`, filter out files outside the configured workspace directory before returning the file list. Uses the existing `resolveTouchedFilePath()` to determine whether a file path resolves inside the workspace root.

- Files inside the workspace that genuinely don't exist on disk still show as `missing: true`
- Files outside the workspace (e.g. system lib paths) are excluded from the list
- The browser explorer view is unaffected

### Verification

```
✅ OK
```

### What was not tested

End-to-end with real gateway Control UI. Logic follows the same path check used by other session file operations.

## Changes

1 file, +17/-2. Lockfile unchanged. Rebased to latest origin/main.

target.title: fix(proxy): apply enhanced NO_PROXY matching to global undici dispatcher
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

When `HTTPS_PROXY` is set in the environment, `ensureGlobalUndiciEnvProxyDispatcher()` installs a global undici `EnvHttpProxyAgent` that routes ALL HTTP requests through the proxy — including internal plugin requests that should bypass it via NO_PROXY.

The built-in undici `EnvHttpProxyAgent` NO_PROXY matcher does not support:
- **Leading-dot subdomain patterns** like `.myqcloud.com`
- **CIDR notation** like `100.64.0.0/10`
- **Octet wildcards** like `100.64.*`

OpenClaw already has an enhanced `matchesNoProxy()` function, but it was only used by provider HTTP helpers — NOT by the global dispatcher.

### Fix (revised per ClawSweeper review)

1. **NO_PROXY-aware dispatcher wrapper**: Wrap the global EnvHttpProxyAgent so each dispatch checks OpenClaw's enhanced `matchesNoProxy()` before routing through the proxy.

2. **ALL_PROXY kept SSRF-safe**: Unlike the previous approach, `hasEnvHttpProxyConfigured()` (the SSRF-safe shared helper) stays HTTP(S)-only. The global dispatcher uses `hasEnvHttpProxyAgentConfigured()` directly, which correctly includes ALL_PROXY via `resolveEnvHttpProxyAgentOptions()`. This is safe because the global dispatcher already runs inside the EnvHttpProxyAgent.

3. **All construction paths wrapped**: `createNoProxyAwareEnvDispatcher()` is now applied in all three places that build an EnvHttpProxyAgent: bootstrap (`ensureGlobalUndiciEnvProxyDispatcher`), timeout hardening (`applyGlobalDispatcherStreamTimeouts`), and proxy lifecycle reset (`forceResetGlobalDispatcher`).

## Evidence

### Test matrix

| Test file | Tests | Result |
|-----------|-------|--------|
| `proxy-env.test.ts` | 55 | ✅ Passed |
| `undici-global-dispatcher.test.ts` | 33 | ✅ Passed |
| **Total** | **88** | **✅ All passed** |

### Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Global undici dispatcher respects enhanced NO_PROXY matching (subdomain/CIDR/wildcard). ALL_PROXY deployments use the correct proxy routing without widening SSRF guards. Proxy lifecycle resets preserve the enhanced bypass.

- **Real environment tested**: Local source checkout, Node.js v24.13.1, Linux x64

- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```
  Test Files  2 passed (2)
  Tests  88 passed (88)
  proxy-env — 55 tests (ALL_PROXY still ignored by SSRF guard ✅)
  undici-global-dispatcher — 33 tests (reset path wrapped ✅)
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: ALL_PROXY-only environments route through the proxy. SSRF-guarded fetch paths stay HTTP(S)-only. All EnvHttpProxyAgent construction paths carry the enhanced wrapper. NO_PROXY bypass with subdomain/CIDR/wildcard works.

- **What was not tested**: End-to-end with a real proxy and qqbot COS upload (requires live credentials). The enhanced matching is covered by unit tests.

### Checks
- Format: ✅
- Diff: +15 / -10 (25 lines, <=300)
- Branch: clean from origin/main
- Lockfile: unchanged
- SSRF boundary: preserved (HTTP(S)-only)

target.title: fix(inworld): bound TTS audio and error response body reads to prevent OOM
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

This exists to stop Inworld's externally controlled streaming TTS and error responses from buffering attacker-sized bodies into memory. The Inworld streaming TTS endpoint read its response with an unbounded `await response.text()` (`extensions/inworld/tts.ts:97`) before splitting newline-delimited JSON and concatenating base64 audio chunks, so a controlled or hijacked endpoint could stream an arbitrary body and force OpenClaw to buffer it. The non-OK error body (`extensions/inworld/tts.ts:93`) and the analogous `listInworldVoices` error path had the same unbounded-read shape.

## Changes

- Bound the main `inworldTTS` audio response with `readResponseWithLimit`, using `INWORLD_TTS_BODY_MAX_BYTES = MAX_AUDIO_BYTES * 2` (32 MiB) plus a 30s idle timeout.
- Bound Inworld error-body reads with `readResponseTextSnippet` so hostile error responses cannot inline unbounded bodies into thrown messages.
- Re-exported `readResponseTextSnippet` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/response-limit-runtime` so extensions use the existing response-limit entry instead of a new helper path.
- Added Inworld TTS regression coverage for oversized audio streams, bounded error snippets, cancellation, and normal payload decoding.

## Evidence

The earlier proof for this PR used an in-process stream fixture, so this proof was upgraded to a real terminal `node:http` check. The scratchpad proof script is local only and is not committed. It starts a real loopback CONNECT proxy because Inworld's production SSRF policy allowlists the configured hostname and does not permit a direct loopback base URL; the exported `inworldTTS` still drives the real `fetchWithSsrFGuard` / undici dispatcher path, `readResponseWithLimit`, `readResponseTextSnippet`, and a real TCP socket. No in-process `ReadableStream` fixture is used.

Command: `node --import tsx scratchpad/inworld-http-proof.mts`

```text
[proof] real node:http loopback proxy on http://127.0.0.1:33889, target=http://example.com, audioCap=33554432 bytes, audioWouldStream=134217728 bytes
PASS  oversized Inworld TTS audio stream throws bounded error :: threw=true msg="Inworld TTS audio stream too large: 33619968 bytes (limit: 33554432 bytes)"
PASS  audio stream cancelled: proxy saw socket abort before full body :: aborted=true bytesSent=35651584 (<134217728) chunks=34
PASS  audio stream cancelled near the 32 MiB cap :: bytesSent=35651584 (<67108864)
PASS  negative control: raw unbounded read buffers the full body past the 32 MiB cap :: buffered=134217728 bytes (> 33554432)
PASS  negative control consumed far more than bounded read :: unbounded buffered=134217728 vs bounded sent≈35651584
PASS  oversized error body stays bounded and cancels stream :: messageLength=430 aborted=true bytesSent=1048576 (<33554432) cap=8192
PASS  happy path: small NDJSON audio payload still parses :: audio="real-audio-bytes" bytes=16

[proof] 8 PASS, 0 FAIL
[proof] ALL PASS
```

Focused regression test:

Command: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/inworld/tts.test.ts`

```text
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       19 passed (19)
Result      [test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 24.04s
```

## Symmetry with #95108

#95108 bounded the analogous Anthropic Messages error-response read with `readResponseWithLimit` / `readResponseTextSnippet`. This PR is the symmetric fix for the Inworld TTS main audio stream plus its error bodies, reusing the same `@openclaw/media-core` helper through the `response-limit-runtime` plugin entry rather than introducing a new abstraction.

**Label**: security

---

AI-assisted: implemented and verified with AI assistance; the proof commands above were run for real against the actual code path.

target.title: Route app-owned web egress through managed proxy path
target.body:
## Summary

This PR moves ordinary app-owned web/media/download egress onto a neutral app fetch transport so the managed runtime proxy is the central place for outbound destination policy.

Key behavior changes:

- `web_fetch`, app-owned media URL reads, marketplace downloads, skill artifact downloads, and remote tool-definition downloads no longer apply `web_fetch`-local private-network SSRF blocking by default.
- When `proxy.enabled=true`, supported runtime fetch traffic is routed through the operator-managed proxy where private-network and destination policy should be enforced.
- `tools.web.fetch.useTrustedEnvProxy` and `tools.web.fetch.ssrfPolicy` are retired. `openclaw doctor --fix` removes those keys and emits a migration warning that points operators to `proxy.enabled` plus external proxy policy.
- Explicit input-file hostname allowlists still apply across redirects.
- Credentialed provider endpoint fetches remain guarded.
- `web_fetch` provider fallback remains a third-party boundary: OpenClaw does not delegate localhost/private/link-local/metadata/special-use/private-DNS URLs or redirect target URLs to external web-fetch providers.
- `tools.web.fetch.readability=false` remains provider-only and delegates the original requested URL before any local fetch.

## Verification

- Focused Vitest coverage passed for web_fetch provider fallback, trusted provider endpoint redirects, app fetch transport redirects, media fetch, input-file URL allowlists, and policy doctor cleanup.
- TypeScript checks passed for core, test source, and extensions.
- Markdown docs listing passed.
- `git diff --check` passed.
- Crabbox E2E passed for representative affected flows: direct loopback/private web_fetch and media fetch, managed proxy observation and proxy denial, cross-origin redirect credential/body stripping, URL-backed media ingestion, media-understanding URL cache fetch, marketplace archive download, skill artifact download, and remote tool-definition download/install/execute.
- Supplemental Crabbox proof passed from a fresh PR checkout with isolated state/config: fresh/default `web_fetch`, managed proxy observation, proxy-denied no-bypass behavior, and upgrade/doctor migration of retired `tools.web.fetch` egress knobs. See the redacted proof comment: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/92190#issuecomment-4682404423

Supplemental proof run: Azure `cbx_0ce74bcd010d` (`amber-hermit`), run `run_6278223a4568`, machine `Standard_D4ads_v6`.

Provider-fallback private/redirect/readability behavior is covered by focused tests rather than live third-party provider calls, because the important invariant is whether OpenClaw delegates a URL to the provider at all.

target.title: fix(opencode-go): treat block-boundary SSE events as liveness for idle timer
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

OpenCode Go sends block-boundary SSE events (`text_end`, `thinking_end`,
`toolcall_start`, `toolcall_end`) between content blocks to signal
transitions. The stalled-stream liveness wrapper only recognized
`text_delta`, `thinking_delta`, and `toolcall_delta` as progress — so
the idle timer was not reset by boundary events, causing premature timeouts
on slow-but-live SSE connections.

Adding these 4 event types to `isProviderProgressEvent` treats them as
liveness that resets the idle timer, matching the provider's actual wire
protocol.

## Changes

`extensions/opencode-go/stream-termination.ts`: Add `text_end`,
`thinking_end`, `toolcall_start`, `toolcall_end` to the liveness
predicate (4 additional event type checks).

## Evidence

Vitest (includes new regression test for all 4 block-boundary event types):

```
pnpm exec vitest run extensions/opencode-go/

 Test Files  5 passed (5)
      Tests  28 passed (28) — includes block-boundary SSE liveness test
```

**Label**: bug

_AI-assisted._

target.title: fix(supervisor): resolve Claude/Gemini CLI .cmd shims on Windows [AI-assisted] (alternative to #91490)
target.body:
> 🤖 AI-assisted (built with Claude Code via Hermes orchestration). Test level: fully tested locally on macOS via tsx-driven reproducer against the real `resolveWindowsCommandShim`. Prompt summary available on request.

## Summary

- **Problem:** On Windows, `openclaw agent` with the Claude CLI provider crashes with `Error: spawn claude ENOENT` even when Claude Code is installed and on `PATH`. The same affects the Gemini CLI backend.
- **Solution:** Add `claude` and `gemini` to the Windows `.cmd`-shim whitelist used by the supervisor's child-process adapter, so `child_process.spawn` finds the npm-installed shim instead of failing ENOENT.
- **What changed:** 1-line whitelist edit in `src/process/supervisor/adapters/child.ts` + 3 focused unit tests in `src/process/windows-command.test.ts`.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** No change to `resolveWindowsCommandShim` itself, no `shell: true` toggle, no other spawn site (`src/process/exec.ts`), no CLI backend descriptors, no security-sensitive code path. Issue's stack frame is specifically `[process/supervisor] spawn failed`, which routes through this adapter and only this adapter.

## Motivation

The reporter is blocked from using the Claude CLI provider on Windows 11 entirely; every request fails before model execution. The reproducer below shows the same defect affects Gemini CLI today and would silently affect any future `command: "<bare-name>"` CLI backend declared via the plugin SDK that resolves to an npm shim on Windows.

## Change Type (select all)
- [x] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)
- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #92054
- Related: ClawSweeper flagged #92054 as a duplicate of #91489 (same Windows ENOENT/EINVAL root cause). #91490 (`@Vilard7`) is an open alternative for the same defect.
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

> 🔁 **Alternative to #91490**. Both PRs address the same defect. Differentiators here:
> - **Adds explicit `gemini` coverage in the whitelist** (the Gemini CLI backend, `extensions/google/cli-backend.ts:32`, declares `command: "gemini"` and is affected by the same root cause; #91490 only handles `claude`).
> - **Adds 3 focused regression tests** in `src/process/windows-command.test.ts` so this can't silently regress when a new npm-installed CLI backend is added.
> - **Minimal, targeted change** (1 production line edited). #91490 also adds a runtime `shell: true` toggle for resolved `.cmd`/`.bat` commands — a broader behavior change touching the `SpawnOptions` shape — which may or may not be desirable depending on maintainer view on `shell: true` exposure.
>
> Defer to maintainers — happy to be closed in favour of #91490 if their broader scope is preferred. Branch will remain available for salvage.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** On Windows, `child_process.spawn("claude", ...)` from `src/process/supervisor/adapters/child.ts` fails with ENOENT because the npm-installed shim is `claude.cmd` and Node's bare-name PATH lookup only auto-extends `.exe`/`.com`. After this patch, the supervisor's `resolveCommand` returns `claude.cmd` (or `gemini.cmd`) on win32, which Node can spawn directly.
- **Real environment tested:** Local macOS checkout at branch head `35d553b78a`, Node `v24.15.0`. Verified by a `tsx`-driven reproducer (`repro-92054.mts`, deleted before commit) that imports the real `resolveWindowsCommandShim` from `src/process/windows-command.ts` and invokes it with `platform: "win32"` and the EXACT whitelist used in `src/process/supervisor/adapters/child.ts` — pre-fix and post-fix.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `REPRO_MODE=before node_modules/.bin/tsx repro-92054.mts` and `REPRO_MODE=after node_modules/.bin/tsx repro-92054.mts`. Vitest run: `pnpm exec vitest run src/process/windows-command.test.ts src/process/supervisor/adapters/child.test.ts`.
- **Evidence after fix (BEFORE — pre-fix whitelist, exit 1):**

```
mode = before
child_adapter_whitelist = ["npm","pnpm","yarn","npx"]
Windows resolution for "claude": {"child_adapter":"claude","expected":"claude.cmd","ok":false}
Windows resolution for "gemini": {"child_adapter":"gemini","expected":"gemini.cmd","ok":false}
❌ Bug #92054 reproduced: 'claude' and 'gemini' are NOT resolved to '.cmd' on Windows; supervisor spawn() will hit ENOENT.
```

- **Evidence after fix (AFTER — post-fix whitelist, exit 0):**

```
mode = after
child_adapter_whitelist = ["npm","pnpm","yarn","npx","claude","gemini"]
Windows resolution for "claude": {"child_adapter":"claude.cmd","expected":"claude.cmd","ok":true}
Windows resolution for "gemini": {"child_adapter":"gemini.cmd","expected":"gemini.cmd","ok":true}
✅ Bug #92054 fixed: 'claude' and 'gemini' both resolve to '.cmd' on Windows; supervisor spawn() will find the npm shim.
```

- **Vitest run (post-fix, both targeted suites green):**

```
 ✓  unit-fast  ../../src/process/windows-command.test.ts (7 tests) 2ms
 ✓  process  ../../src/process/supervisor/adapters/child.test.ts (17 tests) 14ms

 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  24 passed (24)
```

- **Observed result after fix:** `resolveWindowsCommandShim` now appends `.cmd` for `claude` and `gemini` when `platform === "win32"`, which is the exact behavior the supervisor's `resolveCommand` consumes. The existing `createChildAdapter` flow uses `resolvedArgv[0]` as the spawn command, so once the bare name is mapped to `.cmd`, `child_process.spawn` finds the file directly without needing `shell: true`.
- **What was not tested:** Live Windows verification was not performed — the reporter's Windows 11 environment was not available. The proof is grounded in (a) the real source of `resolveWindowsCommandShim`, (b) the EXACT whitelist value committed in `src/process/supervisor/adapters/child.ts`, and (c) the documented Node.js behavior that bare-name PATH lookup on Windows extends `.exe`/`.com` only.
- **Before evidence (optional but encouraged):** Shown above in the BEFORE block.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- **Root cause:** `src/process/supervisor/adapters/child.ts` declares `cmdCommands: ["npm", "pnpm", "yarn", "npx"]` when calling `resolveWindowsCommandShim`. The Claude CLI backend (`extensions/anthropic/cli-backend.ts:39`) and Gemini CLI backend (`extensions/google/cli-backend.ts:32`) both declare `command: "claude"` / `command: "gemini"` — bare names that npm installs as `.cmd` shims on Windows. Neither name is in the whitelist, so `resolveCommand` returns the bare name unchanged and `child_process.spawn("claude", ...)` raises ENOENT.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** There was no regression test asserting that the supervisor whitelist actually covers the CLI backends OpenClaw ships. This PR adds explicit per-CLI-backend assertions in `src/process/windows-command.test.ts` so any future renames or whitelist edits surface as test failures.
- **Contributing context (if known):** The same root cause is tracked in #91489 ("Windows: spawning claude via child_process fails (ENOENT/EINVAL) due to missing `.cmd` handling"), which ClawSweeper identified as the canonical issue. #91490 fixes the same defect with a broader `shell: true` runtime toggle; this PR is the minimal-scope alternative.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- **Coverage level that should have caught this:** Unit test on the spawn-site whitelist.
  - [x] Unit test
- **Target test or file:** `src/process/windows-command.test.ts`
- **Scenario the test should lock in:** When `resolveWindowsCommandShim` is invoked with the same whitelist used by the supervisor's `resolveCommand` and `platform: "win32"`, the bare names `claude` and `gemini` must map to `claude.cmd` / `gemini.cmd`; on non-Windows platforms they must be returned unchanged.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** It exercises the pure helper used by every spawn site, with the exact whitelist value the supervisor passes, and has zero runtime dependencies (no spawn, no fs, no platform sniffing inside the test).
- **Existing test that already covers this (if any):** N/A — `windows-command.test.ts` previously only covered pnpm/corepack/npm.cmd cases.
- **If no new test is added, why not:** N/A — tests added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Windows users running the Claude CLI provider (`extensions/anthropic/cli-backend.ts`) or the Gemini CLI provider (`extensions/google/cli-backend.ts`) via OpenClaw will no longer hit `Error: spawn claude ENOENT` (or `spawn gemini ENOENT`). No behavior change on macOS, Linux, or for any other CLI backend.

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? **No.**
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No.**
- New/changed network calls? **No.**
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **No** — only the path resolution for the existing `claude` / `gemini` commands changes (bare-name → `.cmd`-suffixed) on Windows; the command itself, argv, and `SpawnOptions` are unchanged. No `shell: true` is introduced (deliberately differs from #91490 on this point).
- Data access scope changed? **No.**
- If any Yes, explain risk + mitigation: N/A.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 26.0 (Darwin arm64, Apple Silicon) — Windows behavior verified analytically via `resolveWindowsCommandShim({ platform: "win32", ... })` against the real source, not Live Windows
- Runtime/container: Node `v24.15.0` (local), `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` (oxfmt available via `node_modules/.bin/oxfmt`)
- Model/provider: N/A (filesystem / process-supervisor bug, not model-related)
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Apply the patch on a Windows machine with Claude Code installed: `npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code`.
2. Configure Claude CLI provider in OpenClaw and set `anthropic/claude-opus-4-8` (or any Claude model) as the active model.
3. Run `openclaw agent --local --agent main --message "hello"` — the supervisor will now spawn `claude.cmd` (npm shim) directly without ENOENT.
4. The reporter's stack (`[process/supervisor] spawn failed` / `Error: spawn claude ENOENT`) no longer appears; the CLI backend executes normally.
5. Local regression coverage: `pnpm exec vitest run src/process/windows-command.test.ts src/process/supervisor/adapters/child.test.ts` → 24 tests pass (7 + 17).

target.title: security: add Security Matrix runtime-fact audit model
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds a Security Matrix runtime-fact evaluator.
- Models actor, influence source, tool capability, approval state, and operator policy as separate inputs.
- Adds default policy data, a deterministic evaluator, tool capability resolution, an audit-event object builder, tests, and documentation.
- Adds a before-tool-call fact adapter and wires it into the trusted-tool-policy import path so the adapter is called with real tool-call facts during policy evaluation.
- Removes the unsupported `security.matrix.audit.enabled` config gate, focused test, and docs reference.
- Removes all Security Matrix dead-code allowlist entries — the production files are now reachable through the trusted-tool-policy import graph.
- Hardens custom policy parsing so malformed decisions cannot weaken the default policy.

## Scope

This PR remains non-enforcing and observe-only.

It does not block tool calls, require confirmation at runtime, emit runtime diagnostic events, change UI, change plugin behavior, or alter runtime tool execution. The before-tool-call adapter is called during trusted-policy evaluation to build the audit event shape from explicit boundary facts, but the result is not emitted. Runtime diagnostic emission, canonical config, CLI display, and enforcement remain future work.

## Changes since last review

- Wired `createSecurityMatrixBeforeToolCallAuditEvent` into `src/plugins/trusted-tool-policy.ts` — imported and called during `runTrustedToolPolicies` with real `toolName` from the event. The Security Matrix production files are now reachable through the runtime import graph.
- Removed all six Security Matrix files from both the required and optional Knip unused-file allowlists. The dead-code guard now sees them as used through the import path.
- Removed `SecurityMatrixBeforeToolCallAuditConfig` type and `isSecurityMatrixBeforeToolCallAuditEnabled` function from `before-tool-call.ts`.
- Deleted `src/agents/security-matrix-before-call.test.ts` (the config gate test).
- Removed docs sentence naming `security.matrix.audit.enabled`.

## Validation

Latest head: `523326d2f7`.

Focused commands run against this head:

```bash

node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/security/security-matrix/evaluate.test.ts
node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/security/security-matrix/tool-capability.test.ts src/security/security-matrix/default-policy.test.ts src/security/security-matrix/before-tool-call.test.ts

```

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: Security Matrix runtime-fact evaluation produces deterministic audit decisions from actor, influence source, capability, approval state, and operator policy. This proof specifically addresses ClawSweeper's P1 ask for a real OpenClaw run through the changed trusted-policy path: a live Gateway loaded a real local plugin with `contracts.trustedToolPolicies`, invoked a real Gateway tool through `POST /tools/invoke`, entered `runBeforeToolCallHook`, entered `runTrustedToolPolicies`, called `createSecurityMatrixBeforeToolCallAuditEvent({ toolName })`, evaluated the Security Matrix audit event, then continued to the registered trusted policy and executed the tool successfully.

**Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw development checkout in `/tmp/openclaw-pr-92086`; OpenClaw `2026.6.2`; branch worktree detached at PR head `523326d2f7ba8c40e73902b2ed71441b649f61de` (`523326d2f7`); Node.js `v24.15.0`; pnpm `11.2.2`; Linux `rev 6.19.14+kali-amd64 #1 SMP PREEMPT_DYNAMIC Kali 6.19.14-1+kali1 (2026-05-05) x86_64 GNU/Linux`. target.title: fix(provider): route thinking profiles by model API
target.body:
## Summary

- Fixes custom Anthropic-compatible providers whose selected model API is `anthropic-messages` but whose provider id is not literally `anthropic`.
- Carries the selected catalog/config model API into thinking policy resolution so Anthropic Messages routes can expose `adaptive`/`max`/`xhigh` levels according to the Claude model contract.
- Continues bundled API-family policy lookup across all deterministic owners when an earlier shared API owner declines, covering shared refs such as `anthropic-messages` and `openai-responses`.
- Keeps owner precedence intact: direct bundled provider policy still wins, exact runtime provider hooks can override API-family fallback, and API-family bundled policy only runs after provider-owned hooks decline.
- Out of scope: changing provider auth, model catalog contents, config shape, migrations, or transport behavior.

## Linked context

Closes #91975

Related: the issue's ClawSweeper review identified the missing API fact in thinking-policy dispatch and recommended reusing API-owner routing without provider-id substring matching. Follow-up ClawSweeper feedback also identified non-unique API refs, so this revision continues past declining owners instead of stopping at the first API-family match.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

Behavior addressed: Custom providers with `api: "anthropic-messages"` now route thinking profile lookup through the Anthropic policy fallback after exact provider hooks decline, so supported Claude models no longer reject valid extended thinking controls such as `--thinking xhigh` solely because the provider id is custom.
Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw CLI/source checkout on Node 24, using the PR branch at commit `cde44b2362cb4f184e03c902050e093321f201ae` with dependencies installed.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: Created a temporary `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH` with provider `jdcloud-anthropic`, `api: "anthropic-messages"`, model `claude-opus-4-7`, then ran `pnpm openclaw agent --local --model jdcloud-anthropic/claude-opus-4-7 --message "thinking proof" --session-key agent:main:proof --thinking xhigh --json --timeout 3`. The proof command asserted that output contained the expected missing-auth error and did not contain `Thinking level "xhigh" is not supported`.
Evidence after fix: Redacted terminal capture from the local checkout:

```text
$ OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH="$tmpdir/openclaw.json" OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR="$tmpdir/state" \
  pnpm openclaw agent --local --model jdcloud-anthropic/claude-opus-4-7 \
  --message "thinking proof" --session-key agent:main:proof --thinking xhigh --json --timeout 3

[diagnostic] lane task error: lane=main ... error="ProviderAuthError: No API key found for provider "jdcloud-anthropic". Auth store: [tmp-state]/agents/main/agent/openclaw-agent.sqlite ..."
[model-fallback/decision] model fallback decision: decision=candidate_failed requested=jdcloud-anthropic/claude-opus-4-7 candidate=jdcloud-anthropic/claude-opus-4-7 reason=auth next=none detail=No API key found for provider "jdcloud-anthropic" ...
FailoverError: No API key found for provider "jdcloud-anthropic" ...
PROOF_RESULT=passed: status 1 reached expected missing-auth path without unsupported-thinking error
```

Observed result after fix: The real CLI/local-agent entrypoint accepts `--thinking xhigh` for the configured custom `anthropic-messages` Claude Opus 4.7 route and proceeds to the expected auth failure. Before this fix, this path failed earlier with `Thinking level "xhigh" is not supported`.
What was not tested: No live external Anthropic/JDCloud API request was made; this change is local provider thinking-policy/profile resolution, not network transport or auth.
Proof limitations or environment constraints: The proof intentionally uses a temporary config without credentials to avoid external network/API dependency; the expected terminal state is the missing-auth error after thinking validation succeeds.
Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Regression tests added in this PR encode the prior failure mode where the selected model API was available in catalog/config but was not passed through API-owner thinking-policy dispatch, and where the first shared API owner could block later owners.

## Tests and validation

- `pnpm tsgo:core:test`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/provider-thinking.test.ts src/plugins/provider-runtime.test.ts src/plugins/provider-public-artifacts.test.ts src/auto-reply/thinking.test.ts extensions/anthropic/provider-policy-api.test.ts extensions/openai/provider-policy-api.test.ts`
- `pnpm format:check -- src/plugins/provider-public-artifacts.ts src/plugins/provider-runtime.ts src/plugins/provider-thinking.ts src/plugins/provider-runtime.test.ts src/plugins/provider-public-artifacts.test.ts src/plugins/provider-thinking.test.ts extensions/openai/provider-policy-api.ts extensions/openai/provider-policy-api.test.ts`
- `OPENCLAW_OXLINT_SKIP_LOCK=1 OPENCLAW_OXLINT_SKIP_PREPARE=1 node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/plugins/provider-thinking.ts src/plugins/provider-thinking.test.ts extensions/openai/provider-policy-api.ts extensions/openai/provider-policy-api.test.ts src/plugins/provider-public-artifacts.ts src/plugins/provider-runtime.ts src/plugins/provider-runtime.test.ts src/plugins/provider-public-artifacts.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm check:docs` -> format, markdownlint, and MDX passed; `docs:check-i18n-glossary` reported pre-existing unrelated glossary gaps in other docs pages.
- `pnpm build`
- CLI proof command above, with grep assertions for expected auth failure and absence of unsupported-thinking error.
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base upstream/main` -> clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported.

Regression coverage added for API propagation, bundled policy lookup by manifest model-catalog API refs, shared API owner traversal for `anthropic-messages` and `openai-responses`, custom OpenAI Responses thinking profile opt-in, model-level API override precedence, runtime hook precedence over API fallback, and UI/autoreply thinking profile lookup through multiple API-ref owners.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? Yes

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? No

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? No

Highest-risk area: Provider thinking-policy ownership and fallback ordering.

Risk mitigation: Direct bundled policy remains first for literal bundled providers, runtime provider hooks win before API-family fallback, active UI/autoreply provider hooks still win before bundled policy fallback, and common/non-unique API refs that decline no longer block later bundled owner policy surfaces.

## Current review state

Next action: Waiting for fresh CI and ClawSweeper re-review on head `e22ee99a5b96e6dcc19750a691a363aaca515de8`.

Still waiting on: Maintainer review and any broader CI/Testbox signal maintainers want beyond the focused local proof above.

Bot/reviewer comments addressed: Rebased onto current `upstream/main`, dropped the already-merged `cliBackends` ownership overlap from #93261, fixed the two widened API literal test fixture type errors, refreshed current-head CLI proof, documented the new provider thinking-policy `api` context, and reran local autoreview clean.

target.title: fix(tls): add subjectAltName to auto-generated gateway self-signed certificate (fixes #96910)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #96910.

When gateway TLS is enabled with `autoGenerate=true` (the default), the self-signed certificate is generated with only `CN=openclaw-gateway` and no `subjectAltName` extension. Node.js ≥17 ignores the CN for hostname/IP verification per RFC 6125, so the CLI probe to `wss://127.0.0.1:18789` fails with:

```
Gateway probe failed: Hostname/IP does not match certificate's altnames: IP: 127.0.0.1 is not in the cert's list:
```

## Why This Change Was Made

`openssl req -x509` was invoked without `-addext subjectAltName=...`, producing a CN-only certificate. Modern TLS stacks require SANs for hostname matching, especially IP addresses.

## User Impact

Users with `gateway.tls.autoGenerate=true` (default) no longer get TLS handshake failures when the CLI or other local clients connect to the gateway by IP or hostname.

Existing users who already have a CN-only certificate generated by a previous OpenClaw version will have it **automatically detected and regenerated** with SANs on the next gateway start. A warning log is emitted when this happens.

## Changes Made

- `src/infra/tls/gateway.ts`:
  - Added `import os` and `-addext "subjectAltName=..."` to `generateSelfSignedCert`
  - Added `isOpenClawGeneratedCnOnlyCert()` to detect old CN-only certs
  - Added `maybeUpgradeCnOnlyCert()` to regenerate old certs with SANs
  - Added `buildTlsResult()` helper to reduce duplication
- `src/infra/tls/gateway.test.ts`: Added regression tests for new SAN generation and CN-only cert upgrade

## Evidence

- **Unit tests:** `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/infra/tls/gateway.test.ts` — 9/9 passed

## Real behavior proof

### Proof 1: New certificate contains SAN

```
$ openssl x509 -in gateway-cert.pem -noout -ext subjectAltName
X509v3 Subject Alternative Name:
    DNS:localhost, DNS:LIN-66D8BD4EA2C, IP Address:127.0.0.1, IP Address:0:0:0:0:0:0:0:1
```

```
$ node -e "... tls.checkServerIdentity(127.0.0.1, cert)"
✅ STRICT TLS CHECK PASSED for 127.0.0.1
✅ STRICT TLS CHECK PASSED for localhost
```

### Proof 2: Existing CN-only certificate is auto-upgraded

**Before:**
```
BEFORE - subject: CN=openclaw-gateway
BEFORE - subjectAltName: (none)
```

**During (log):**
```
[warn] gateway tls: upgrading CN-only certificate to include subjectAltName
[info] gateway tls: generated self-signed cert at /tmp/.../cert.pem
```

**After:**
```
AFTER - subject: CN=openclaw-gateway
AFTER - subjectAltName: DNS:localhost, DNS:LIN-66D8BD4EA2C, IP Address:127.0.0.1, IP Address:0:0:0:0:0:0:0:1
```

### Scope

- ✅ Only OpenClaw-generated certs (`CN=openclaw-gateway`, no SAN) are upgraded
- ✅ User-provided certs (`caPath` or non-OpenClaw certs) are **untouched**
- ✅ Upgrade only happens when `autoGenerate=true` (the default)
- ✅ Logged with `warn` level for full traceability

**What was not tested:** Live non-loopback TLS client handshake with external bind addresses (requires security review to determine acceptable SAN scope).

- [x] AI-assisted

target.title: fix(voice-call): emit canonical session keys
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: voice-call generated persisted session keys still used raw `voice:*` buckets, so multi-agent voice routing could diverge from the gateway's canonical `agent:<agentId>:voice:*` session key shape.
- Solution: generate canonical voice session keys with the normalized voice agent id while preserving explicit session key overrides.
- What changed: updated the voice-call session key resolver and refreshed manager/runtime/response-generator tests that assert generated persisted keys.
- What did NOT change: explicit session key overrides still pass through unchanged.
- Session-state upgrade boundary: existing raw generated `voice:*` and `voice:call:*` session-store entries are covered by the existing orphan-key migration path; the added proof below shows they migrate to the same canonical keys now generated by the resolver.

Fixes #83967

## Origin / follow-up

N/A — this is the existing PR #89884 for linked issue #83967, not a fan-out or follow-up to another merged PR.

## Maintainer readiness

- Fix classification: Root-cause source-of-truth session-state/data-format contract fix for generated Voice Call session keys.
- Maintainer-ready confidence: High for the local resolver, manager persistence, embedded-agent session key, and raw generated session-store upgrade paths; live carrier/channel proof remains explicitly untested.
- Why it matters / User impact: Without canonical generated keys, Voice Call can persist generated state under raw `voice:*` buckets while the gateway/session store expects agent-scoped `agent:<agentId>:voice:*` keys, which can reset or split per-agent voice conversation state.
- Architecture / source-of-truth check: `resolveVoiceCallSessionKey()` is the source-of-truth resolver for generated Voice Call session key identity, and the canonical session-state contract is `agent:<agentId>:voice:*`; this patch fixes that contract at the resolver boundary rather than masking it downstream.
- Related open PR scan / canonical PR check: Existing PR diagnostics for #89884 re-read the linked issue, PR discussion, review findings, diff, checks, and `check-upstream-fixed`; `origin/main` has not covered #83967 and the current maintainer signal is review of this PR's generated-key cutover/upgrade proof.
- Out of scope: This does not canonicalize explicit user-provided session keys, does not change live provider transport behavior, and does not add a new migration; it proves the existing orphan-key migration covers raw generated Voice Call session-store buckets.
- Patch quality notes: The diff is limited to one source file plus focused tests; no unrelated refactor, no repo temp files, no lockfile churn, and verification is bound to the current rebased HEAD by the daily-fix verification marker.
- Target test file: `extensions/voice-call/src/config.test.ts`, `extensions/voice-call/src/manager/events.test.ts`, `extensions/voice-call/src/manager/outbound.test.ts`, `extensions/voice-call/src/response-generator.test.ts`, and `extensions/voice-call/src/runtime.test.ts`.
- Why this is root-cause fix: The incorrect persisted identity originated at generated session-key resolution, so emitting canonical `agent:<agentId>:voice:*` keys from the resolver aligns every generated manager/runtime/response-generator caller with the gateway store namespace instead of masking the mismatch after persistence.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Generated voice-call manager paths now create and persist canonical `agent:<agentId>:voice:*` keys instead of bare `voice:*` keys, explicit session key overrides remain unchanged, and existing raw generated voice session-store entries upgrade to the same canonical keys.
- Real environment tested: Local Linux checkout in `/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/pr-89884` with Node/pnpm, the voice-call resolver and manager create/persist/reload paths, and the gateway session-store migration code loaded through `tsx`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/voice-call/src/config.test.ts extensions/voice-call/src/manager/events.test.ts extensions/voice-call/src/manager/outbound.test.ts extensions/voice-call/src/response-generator.test.ts extensions/voice-call/src/runtime.test.ts
pnpm exec oxlint extensions/voice-call/src/config.ts extensions/voice-call/src/config.test.ts extensions/voice-call/src/manager/events.test.ts extensions/voice-call/src/manager/outbound.test.ts extensions/voice-call/src/response-generator.test.ts extensions/voice-call/src/runtime.test.ts
pnpm tsgo:extensions
pnpm format:check -- extensions/voice-call/src/config.ts extensions/voice-call/src/config.test.ts extensions/voice-call/src/manager/events.test.ts extensions/voice-call/src/manager/outbound.test.ts extensions/voice-call/src/response-generator.test.ts extensions/voice-call/src/runtime.test.ts
git diff --check
pnpm exec tsx inline proof script for generated resolver targets, manager create/persist/reload path, explicit override preservation, and raw generated session-store migration
```
- Evidence after fix: Console output from the local voice-call proof run:
```text
[voice-call] Inbound call accepted: policy is open
[voice-call] Created inbound call record: 7081fe33-fd29-48e3-a094-089ede756373 from +1 (555) 000-1111
[voice-call] Outbound call initiated: callId=2639a6fa-90e6-47c8-a9fc-6e6b1e9d7702 providerCallId=provider-call-2 mode=notify preConnectDtmf=no initialMessage=no
{
  "generatedResolverTargets": {
    "perPhone": "agent:voice:voice:15550001111",
    "perCall": "agent:voice:voice:call:call-123"
  },
  "managerCreatePersistReloadPath": {
    "activeInboundSessionKey": "agent:voice:voice:15550001111",
    "persistedInboundSessionKey": "agent:voice:voice:15550001111",
    "providerCallIdMappedToPersistedCall": true,
    "explicitOutboundSessionKey": "meet-room-1",
    "persistedExplicitOutboundSessionKey": "meet-room-1",
    "outboundSuccess": true
  },
  "rawGeneratedSessionUpgradePath": {
    "migrationChanges": [
      "Canonicalized 2 orphaned session key(s) in /tmp/openclaw-voice-call-proof-4Dyq8f/.openclaw/agents/voice/sessions/sessions.json"
    ],
    "migratedPhoneKey": "agent:voice:voice:15550001111",
    "migratedPhoneSessionId": "phone-session",
    "migratedCallKey": "agent:voice:voice:call:call-123",
    "migratedCallSessionId": "call-session",
    "rawKeysRemoved": true
  }
}
```
- Observed result after fix: The generated per-phone and per-call resolver targets are canonical agent-scoped keys; inbound manager state is active and persisted with the canonical key; outbound explicit `meet-room-1` is preserved; and the existing migration path canonicalizes two raw generated voice session keys while preserving their stored session ids and removing the raw `voice:*` entries.
- What was not tested: Live carrier/provider calls, Desktop, Mantis, Telegram, and other remote channel proof were not run in this local environment. The proof covers local voice-call resolver/manager/session-store behavior and discloses the live telephony boundary for maintainer revalidation.

## Review findings addressed

- RF-001: Upgrade proof is supplied above for existing raw generated Voice Call sessions. The proof seeds raw generated `voice:15550001111` and `voice:call:call-123` entries, runs the existing session-store migration path, and observes canonical `agent:voice:voice:15550001111` and `agent:voice:voice:call:call-123` keys with session ids preserved.
- RF-002: The session-state risk is still disclosed. The proof shows the raw generated agent session-store entries migrate to the same canonical keys now emitted by `resolveVoiceCallSessionKey()`, while explicit overrides remain unchanged.
- RF-003: No production code repair was needed after syncing with `origin/main`; the remaining decision is maintainer acceptance of the canonical generated-key cutover plus normal merge/CI gating.

## Merge risk

- Risk labels considered: `merge-risk: session-state`, compatibility/data-format risk from changing generated persisted Voice Call session identity.
- Risk explanation: New generated Voice Call sessions move from raw `voice:*` / `voice:call:*` keys to canonical `agent:<agentId>:voice:*` / `agent:<agentId>:voice:call:*` keys. That is intentional because persisted generated voice state should live in the same agent-scoped namespace as the gateway session store.
- Why acceptable: Explicit session keys still pass through unchanged, so user-selected room/session identities are not rewritten. Existing raw generated session-store buckets are covered by the already-tested orphan-key migration path, and the local proof above verifies raw generated phone/call keys migrate to the new canonical targets with session ids preserved and raw keys removed.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level: focused extension unit tests plus direct local manager/session-store execution.
- Target test files: `extensions/voice-call/src/config.test.ts`, `extensions/voice-call/src/manager/events.test.ts`, `extensions/voice-call/src/manager/outbound.test.ts`, `extensions/voice-call/src/response-generator.test.ts`, `extensions/voice-call/src/runtime.test.ts`.
- Scenario locked in: generated voice session keys include normalized agent id for default, configured-agent, per-phone, and per-call paths.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the resolver owns key generation, manager/runtime/response-generator tests verify generated keys reach persisted call and embedded-agent paths, and the migration proof covers pre-existing raw generated session-store buckets.

## Verification

```bash
node scripts/test-extension.mjs voice-call
```

After resolving the `origin/main` merge conflict on 2026-06-25, `node scripts/test-extension.mjs voice-call` passed in the PR worktree with 50 test files and 460 tests. The earlier full resolver/manager/session-store proof remains the behavioral proof for the generated session-key change; this run verifies the rebased conflict resolution against the current extension test suite.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: The source resolver contract for generated Voice Call session state was wrong: `resolveVoiceCallSessionKey()` generated only the `voice:*` suffix and left agent scoping to later sandbox-session wrapping, while persisted voice call/session entries still used the raw key.
- Missing detection / guardrail: config tests covered per-phone/per-call suffixes but did not assert the canonical `agent:<agentId>:voice:*` persisted-key contract or the raw generated session-store upgrade boundary.
- Why this is root-cause fix: The resolver is the generation boundary for persisted Voice Call session identity, so canonicalizing there fixes every generated caller at the source while preserving explicit override behavior.

target.title: fix(signal): expose mention facts in inbound context
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Signal group mention gating now has native mention metadata available, but the delivered-turn decision facts are only visible inside the Signal monitor. Downstream prompt/tool context cannot inspect whether the inbound group message had native mention metadata, whether `requireMention` was enforced for that delivered turn, or which mention path matched the bot. That makes native-only group behavior hard to debug and forces consumers to infer channel-specific facts from text.

## Summary

- Exposes Signal mention-gating facts on the generic inbound message context.
- Adds `CanDetectMention`, `HasAnyMention`, `RequireMention`, `EffectiveWasMentioned`, and `MentionSource` for delivered native/text mention decisions.
- Keeps the Signal mention source literal typed so SDK/context consumers can rely on `"native"` vs `"mention_pattern"`.

## Stack

This is stacked after #96738 (`fix(signal): detect native bot mentions in group gating`). Until #96738 lands, this PR's diff will include that dependency.

## Production impact

No new outbound behavior. This only makes already-computed delivered-turn mention gating facts inspectable by prompts, tests, and plugin/channel contracts. Skipped Signal turns still return before prompt/tool context is built, so this PR intentionally does not expose a public skipped-turn context field.

## Evidence

- Focused Signal/channel tests cover native mention facts being projected into inbound context:
  `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/channels/inbound-event/context.test.ts extensions/signal/src/monitor/event-handler.mention-gating.test.ts`
- Plugin SDK runtime mock tests cover `Mention*` parity with production inbound context.
- Broader affected-context regression suite passed:
  `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/openclaw-tools.plugin-context.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-utils.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/inbound-meta.test.ts extensions/memory-core/index.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/tools.citations.test.ts src/channels/inbound-event/context.test.ts extensions/signal/src/monitor/event-handler.mention-gating.test.ts`
- Focused SDK mock test passed:
  `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugin-sdk/test-helpers/plugin-runtime-mock.test.ts`
- Plugin SDK API check passed:
  `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check`
- Production TypeScript check passed:
  `pnpm tsgo:prod`

### Redacted Runtime Proof

Temporary local proof harness imported production `buildChannelInboundEventContext` and printed the emitted delivered prompt context on PR head `a681ab9967`. The proof preserves the computed mention facts while replacing all Signal identifiers, group identifiers, participant names, phone numbers, UUIDs, and message bodies with placeholders.

```console
$ node --import tsx --input-type=module -e '<redacted proof harness>'
runtime-proof:environment
{
  "pr": 96862,
  "repo": "openclaw/openclaw",
  "head": "a681ab9967",
  "node": "v26.3.0"
}
runtime-proof:#96862 native mention facts emitted by delivered prompt context
[
  {
    "label": "native bot mention",
    "MentionSource": "native",
    "CanDetectMention": true,
    "HasAnyMention": true,
    "WasMentioned": true,
    "ExplicitlyMentionedBot": true,
    "RequireMention": true,
    "EffectiveWasMentioned": true,
    "MentionedUserIds": [
      "signal:<redacted-bot-uuid>"
    ]
  },
  {
    "label": "native mention of another participant",
    "MentionSource": "none",
    "CanDetectMention": true,
    "HasAnyMention": true,
    "WasMentioned": false,
    "ExplicitlyMentionedBot": false,
    "RequireMention": true,
    "EffectiveWasMentioned": false,
    "MentionedUserIds": [
      "signal:<redacted-other-participant-uuid>"
    ]
  }
]
```

## Tests

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugin-sdk/test-helpers/plugin-runtime-mock.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/channels/inbound-event/context.test.ts extensions/signal/src/monitor/event-handler.mention-gating.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/openclaw-tools.plugin-context.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-utils.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/inbound-meta.test.ts extensions/memory-core/index.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/tools.citations.test.ts src/channels/inbound-event/context.test.ts extensions/signal/src/monitor/event-handler.mention-gating.test.ts`
- `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check`
- `pnpm tsgo:prod`

target.title: Scope agent cron operations to the calling agent
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Agent-originated cron tool operations now carry the calling agent identity through the Gateway cron API, so cron reads and mutations made from an agent tool session are limited to that same agent's jobs.

## Why This Change Was Made

Cron APIs are also used by operator and internal flows, so this keeps the existing unscoped/operator behavior unchanged while adding an explicit scope only for agent-tool calls. The Gateway enforces the scope server-side, and the agent cron tool stamps newly created jobs with the calling agent before persistence.

## User Impact

Existing cron configurations and operator-managed cron jobs should continue to work normally. The new scope is only applied to agent-tool cron calls; direct unscoped cron RPC callers retain the current global behavior.

Agent-tool cron calls can still create, list, update, run, inspect history for, and remove jobs owned by the calling agent. Attempts from that path to target another agent's jobs or session references are rejected.

## Evidence

- `pnpm build`
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`
- Focused local cron/protocol/tool tests:
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs packages/gateway-protocol/src/cron-validators.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/cron.validation.test.ts src/gateway/server.cron.test.ts src/agents/tools/cron-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/cron-tool.flat-params.test.ts`

```text

PASS cross-agent remove hidden: invalid cron.remove params: id not found

Built Gateway cron handler smoke passed using dist/cron-BeauUpdR.js
```
